- **Message-ID:** `<IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net...
>
> Today I was grabbed by an enthusiastic person in my workplace who had used 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> easily without any special knowledge.)

    This is a minor gripe of mine, so feel free to ignore this post if the 
issue of standards-conformance and HTML does not interest you.

    Bad HTML versus good HTML might be subjective, but I dislike invalid 
(non-standard conforming) HTML.

From http://www.webstandards.org/about/
<quote>
The Problem

Though leading browser makers have been involved in the creation of web 
standards since W3C was formed, for many years compliance was observed in 
the breach. By releasing browsers that failed to uniformly support 
standards, manufacturers needlessly fragmented the Web, injuring designers, 
developers, users, and businesses alike.

Lack of uniform support for W3C standards left consumers frustrated: when 
using the ï¿½wrongï¿½ browser, many could not view content or perform desired 
transactions. Among those most frequently hurt were people with disabilities 
or special needs.

Quandaries and Costs

At the same time, lack of uniform support for W3C standards left designers, 
developers and site owners in a terrible quandary: could they afford to 
implement multiple versions of every web page in order to accommodate 
incompatible browsers? If not, which browsers should they neglect, and how 
many millions of potential visitors were they willing to turn away? Either 
way, the cost was too high. It still is.
</quote>

    It is not enough for the website to simply "look okay" on any one 
particular browser; the HTML should be structurally correct, and the tags 
used for their semantics, rather than how they render in one particular 
browser. The <em> tag, for example, stands for "emphasis", and using it to 
render text in italics because that's how your particular browser visually 
renders emphasis is incorrect.

    For example, perhaps you have a style guide which says quotation 
attributions should be displayed in italics. You should note write the HTML 
as:

    "Bla bla bla" - <em>some guy</em>

    One reason is that a different browser might not render them as italics; 
another reason is that browsers for the visually impaired will interpret the 
HTML to mean that "some guy" should be spoken with emphasis, clearly giving 
the complete wrong effect to those users.

From 
http://www.law.com/servlet/ContentServer?pagename=OpenMarket/Xcelerate/View&c=LawArticle&cid=1032128683422&t=LawArticleTech
<quote>
When Robert Gumson logs on to the Internet, he uses a software program that 
converts Web site content into speech. But when he logged on to Southwest 
Airlines' Web site to make a reservation, Gumson, who is blind, found that 
the site was incompatible with his screen-reader program.

So Gumson and a Miami Beach, Fla.-based disability rights group, Access Now, 
filed lawsuits in U.S. District Court in Miami in June and July against 
Dallas-based Southwest and Dallas-based American Airlines under the 
Americans with Disabilities Act.
</quote>

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: HTML (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2006-03-02T23:06:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lUKNf.86659$sa3.61137@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13>`

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> 
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> (non-standard conforming) HTML.

Oliver, I can't address HTML but as a golden rule, regardless of which 
language/tool you use there MUST BE a STANDARD - otherwise you finish up 
with those inconsistencies you cited. 'KNOWN' and publicized extensions 
to a Standard are a different thing all together.

As an example, while OO COBOL was still in the embryonic stage, Micro 
Focus produced their version of OO which, to the best of my knowledge, 
was consistent with the standard we now have. One slight difference - 
M/F came up with the following syntax :-

Class-id.	MyClass inherits from.........

Class-Control.	ThisIsClass1	class is "class1"
		ThisisClass2	class is "class2".

The eventual standard used the syntax 'Repository' instead of 
'Class-Control', which Fujitsu had from Day One. Net Express V 4.0 will 
accept both 'Class-Control' and 'Repository', (one of them but not both 
in the same program). Now that's a known difference and given a source 
using 'Class-Control' it wouldn't compile in Fujitsu - an error would be 
highlighted and you change the source to 'Repository' syntax. If you are 
an F/J user taking code from somebody else you may not be aware of 
'Class-Control'. No big deal - the compile is going to red flag it for 
you anyway.

As part of their design M/F added additional syntax to the line :-

Class-id.	MyClass inherits from.........

I wont go into the details but a nifty feature was that you can code 
that to indicate that the Global Data in the first class can be 
referenced by sub-classes. I don't know, but in all probability M/F 
submitted this feature to J4 - sour grapes or bureaucracy, J4 didn't 
pick up on it - even though the chairman was an M/F employee ! Now as of 
V 4, M/F is consistent with the standard but still retained their 
additional features which are EXTENSIONS. In this particular case, 
definitely a problem if a developer using N/E includes them and 
re-thinking if picked up by an F/J developer - although again they would 
be flagged as F/J compile errors.

I mentioned recently a review of Table Syntax in COBOL which is 'occurs 
x times' or 'occurs from a to b depending on c'. John Piggott - I met 
him only once at Newbury (M/F UK HQ), for a J4 meeting in 2000, sat next 
to our Robert Jones who used to trundle up from Gloucester, not as a 
member, but as a developer contributing ideas. John runs a software 
house in Wimbledon where he also travelled from, for his sessions. His 
major interest back then was the Report Writer. I also assumed, from his 
knowledge that he was a J4 member. Not so. Only recently Chuck Stevens 
advised that John was a visitor just like Robert.

Perhaps it's because I was born a Brit that I sense 'Anglo' feelings, 
bearing in mind most of the 12 at the meeting were from this side of the 
Pond. John was the one who submitted a very detailed paper about 
flexible tables (in PROCEDURAL syntax) to attain the changeable 'sizing' 
as already exists in OO lists/collections/maps, regardless of language. 
As the nit-picking brigade got busy on his paper, probably not for the 
first time, my antennae went up, 'John is getting real pissed at this 
nonsense'. I was totally disinterested in the topic, (not being OO), 
don't know what significant changes they made to his proposal, and 
possibly John went home with the thought, "Yeah, I proposed a horse and 
they come up with a bloody camel !'.

Above is not totally germane but shows the other side of the coin when 
determining standards.

Fortunately with Java it is still largely down to Calgary-born and 
educated James Gosling and the Sun team. Woe betide anybody who upsets 
Mr. Gosling - word is he has an extensive vocabulary of expletives !

If you and I go into Tim Hortons for our coffee and doughnut - that's 
what we expect. If some other jerk opens another franchise down the road 
'Krispy Kreme' perhaps, and we order a coffee and danish - we will not 
be thrilled with the thought that the lidded coffee container has orange 
juice, when we take our first sip !

It is absolutely BLOODY RIDICULOUS that somebody would design a version 
of HTML which screws up on :-

 >    "Bla bla bla" - <em>some guy</em>

Taking COBOL as an example :-

	ADD A TO BE GIVING C

That's *exactly* what we expect to happen - and unless he is a space 
cadet, a developer does not expect the result in variable C to contain 
the Square Root or a Factorial ! Want to 'enhance' the language/tool - 
don't *substitute* but come up with an *extension*.

Don't try and control people from making extensions, PROVIDING they are 
documented - at some future date a standards body might see the efficacy 
of incorporating that into an updated standard.

However there is a downside with a product like HTML as you illustrated 
- how does your Browser get around identifying standard entries and 
extensions ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: HTML (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-03-02T21:37:27-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4d089$4407b9f6$45491d7a$26289@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<lUKNf.86659$sa3.61137@pd7tw1no>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13> <lUKNf.86659$sa3.61137@pd7tw1no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> It is absolutely BLOODY RIDICULOUS that somebody would design a version 
> of HTML which screws up on :-
> 
>  >    "Bla bla bla" - <em>some guy</em>

One thing that most developers of HTML (and most all the automated 
generating tools) don't take into account is non-visual or non-graphical 
media representations of documents.  In the HTML and XHTML standards, 
the W3C has addressed other methods of rendering documents.

The Oscars are coming up on Sunday.  Let's suppose I want to express my 
opinion about one of the films up for Best Picture...

Man, <i>Crash</i> has <em>got to</em> win!

Now, visually, how would that render?  In most browsers, both "Crash" 
and "got to" would be rendered in italics (I believe - I hardly ever use 
<em>).  But, in a text-to-speech browser, the italics may be understood 
as a proper name, whereas the speech would probably emphasize "got to".

(Note that the above is an example - I have seen none of the films up 
for Best Picture.)

> However there is a downside with a product like HTML as you illustrated 
> - how does your Browser get around identifying standard entries and 
> extensions ?

I don't think that extensions in HTML are good.  There are a couple of 
standards.  HTML, XHTML, XML, and CSS are four that, for consistency in 
web programming, it is crucial that all user agents render properly. 
However, we've all seen the bug that's promoted to a feature...  :) 
That's what happened.

I had a website where I had a 3-color line at the bottom, before the 
"page design by [blahblahblah]" stuff.  In Firefox, Mozilla, and 
Konqueror, I did this with 3 <hr> elements, back to back.

<hr style="border:solid 5px navy;" />
<hr style="border:solid 5px white;" />
<hr style="border:solid 5px darkgreen;" />

In IE, this same setup *double-spaced* the lines!  :(  I ended up having 
to nest <div> elements - which works, but it's crap.  (This is what I 
did...)

<div style="border-top:solid 5px navy;">
     <div style="border-top:solid 5px white;">
         <div style="border-top:solid 5px darkgreen;">
             [content]
         </div>
     </div>
</div>

I can second Mr. Wong's contention that the different way browsers 
render HTML is *very* frustrating for web developers.
```

LX-i wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> union requirements...  heh...

I suppose you could say unions, but more like money and some busy savvy 
up here to provide a movie infrastructure, and seducing Hollywood to 
accept the scenery. Bit meaningless to me, but this is supposed to be an 
exhaustive list, many which I don't even recognize :-

http://www.albertafilm.ca/web/productions_highlights.php

But it misses out on 'Legend of the Falls' - Anthony Hopkins, Brad Pitt, 
Aidan Quinn. Kevin Costner *almost* filmed 'Dances with Wolves' up here 
- southern scenery very comparqtive to the Dakotas. Don't recall names 
but Kevin Costner and Robert Duval in a movie last year; subsequently 
Duval was up here making I think, a TV mini-series, cattle being taken 
across the States and he picks up a group of abused Chinese women who 
join his cattle party.

There's a guy goes scouting around Alberta looking for likely spots, 
countryside, towns and buildings. Did that in a large swathe west and 
south of Calgary. One of the glossies in to-day's paper runs an article 
on 'Brokeback' with shots of scenery, Kananaskis Country, Fort McLeod, 
Dinton (never heard of the place), Crossfield, Rockyford. For 
'Brokeback' they required a flock of sheep. To keep the park people 
happy they even put down a carpet that the sheep could graze and poop 
on. Shooting over, herd the sheep, remove poop so that local wildlife 
wouldn't get contaminated. Sounds absurd - but worth the bucks :-)
Even final scenes in located in Mexico, were shot here in Alberta.

> I really have no desire to see it.  I remember the movie _Kiss of the 
> Spider Woman_ - sounds like this is just a little less artsy and a 
> little more mainstream.
> 

Don't think I've seen it either; but may have. 'Artsy' - well the 
English expression is 'artsy-fartsy'. 'fartsy' might be more appropriate 
for 'Brokeback' :-). Remains to be seen what real numbers ($'s) it 
brings in once to-night's nonsense of, "I thank my Mom, Her Mom, Her 
Grand-Mom and Her Great-Great-Granddad and my wunnerful understanding 
kids", is over.

As I recall there was a movie called 'Philadelphia' in '94 that won big 
time. Natch, the theme song by Springstein won an Oscar. 10 bucks to the 
first person who can hum the tune 'Philadelphia' !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: HTML (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-03T15:47:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KyZNf.8642$Ui.103@edtnps84>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13> <lUKNf.86659$sa3.61137@pd7tw1no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:lUKNf.86659$sa3.61137@pd7tw1no...
> It is absolutely BLOODY RIDICULOUS that somebody would design a version of 
> HTML which screws up on :-
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> *substitute* but come up with an *extension*.

    Right, the example above, with the <em> tag (for emphasis), comes from 
web developers inapporpriately depending on undocumented features. That is, 
the standard says that the <em> tag should be used for emphasizing text.

    But let's say I don't actually read the standards, but just try out the 
<em> tag to see what it does, and I notice that on my particular browser, 
the <em> tag displays the text in italics (because the browser implementor 
decided that italics is a good way to emphasize text, for example). I might 
say "Oh, so the <em> tag just makes text appear in italics", and then start 
depending on the fact that it'll render in italics. Maybe in a future 
version of the browser, the browser implementor will decide "maybe rendering 
the text in bold is a better indication of emphasis", and so they change it 
so that <em> now causes text to appear in bold. So now my HTML page may be 
broken, for example, if it contained text like this:

--------------------------------------

    Please type in the text below in italics exactly as they appear.

<em>ADD a TO b GIVING c</em>

--------------------------------------

    And then someone viewing this page will not see ANY text in italics at 
all; only text in bold, and might become very confused.

>
> Don't try and control people from making extensions, PROVIDING they are 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> extensions ?

    I believe the HTML standard specifies that browsers should always simply 
ignore any tags that they don't understand. This is to allow potentially 
infinite forward compatibility. For example, I could have an HTML document 
as follows:

--------------------------------------

Please use the following Java applet to calculate your shipping fees

<applet code="shippingCalculator.jar">
Unfortunately, your browser does not seem to support applets. You can 
download Sun's JVM from http://java.sun/com/
</applet>

--------------------------------------

    The idea is that browsers who do not recognize the <applet> tag will 
simply ignore that tag, and display the text without any special formatting 
(for all it knows, perhaps the <applet> tag is supposed to cause the text to 
appear in purple on yellow font, underlined).

    Browsers that DO recognize the applet tag understand that the 
appropriate interpretation is actually to not render any content between the 
opening and closing tag at all (so these browsers will not display the 
"Unfortunately, your etc." message), and instead to download the 
"shippingCalculator.jar" file, and execute it as an applet.

    However, the W3C (World Wide Web Consortium), strongly discourages 
browsers from producing extensions, because they argue it will create a 
fragmented web, where certain pages can only be used by browser A, and other 
pages can only be used by browser B, whereas the goal of the web is to be 
accessible to everyone with an Internet connection.

    - Oliver
```

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-06T17:58:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%K_Of.15255$M52.5376@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13> <lUKNf.86659$sa3.61137@pd7tw1no> <KyZNf.8642$Ui.103@edtnps84> <1141424345.362288.320150@t39g2000cwt.googlegroups.com> <59459$440a6e78$45491d7a$24910@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:59459$440a6e78$45491d7a$24910@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Alistair wrote:
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's right - isn't it <object> now?

    Yes I discovered this recently (maybe a week or two ago) as I've started 
developing applets for fun again. I used the applet tag as an example for 
simplicity. The XHTML1.1 code would look something like this:

    <!--[if !IE]>-->
      <object classid="KanaTest.class" type="application/x-java-applet" 
archive="KanaTest.jar" width="640px" height="300px">
    <!--<![endif]-->
        <object classid="clsid:8AD9C840-044E-11D1-B3E9-00805F499D93" 
height="640px" width="300px" >
          <param name="code" value="KanaTest" />
          <param name="archive" value="KanaTest.jar" />
        </object>
    <!--[if !IE]>-->
      </object>
    <!--<![endif]-->

    Where the "if !IE" nonsense happens to do with the fact that IE does 
things differently than other browsers. So there are two variants of hte 
object tag, with the outer one being for non-IE browsers, while the inner 
one is for IE.

    The <!-- and --> mark the beginning and end of a comment respectively, 
but IE will scan inside the comments to see if there any "browser 
directives". So most browsers will ignore those conditionals, but IE won't.

    So the conditionals tell IE to ignore the outter <object> tag, and so IE 
only sees the inner one.

    Meanwhile, other browsers see the outter tag, and know that they should 
ignore everything inside the tag (and so they ignore the inner <object> 
tag).

    The philosophy of the non-IE version of the <object> tag is you just 
specify the content type ("application/x-java-applet" in this case), and the 
browser will use any plugin it has installed which can render that content 
type.

    The philosophy of the IE version of the <object> tag is that you give a 
unique identifier to the exact plugin executable needed 
("clsid:8AD9C840-044E-11D1-B3E9-00805F499D93" in this case), and the browser 
will automatically download and install it if it isn't already present.

    - Oliver
```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:b0732$440cdad0$45491d7a$389@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Oliver Wong wrote:
>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
[snip]
>
> That *sucks*!  Are you doing that to avoid the |\/|$ JVM handling your 
> applet, or do you have to do it that way?  (If it's the latter, I wonder 
> if IE7 works any closer to the standard?)

    If I put only the non-IE version, then IE doesn't display an applet at 
all. If I put only the IE version, then FireFox doesn't display the applet 
at all.

    I think IE and FireFox simply have two different ideas of semantics of 
the child-nodes and attributes are. For example, FireFox thinks that the 
reference to the JAR file that packages the applet should be in the 
"archive" attribute, while IE thinks it should be in a <PARAM> node.

    - Oliver
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **In-Reply-To:** `<KyZNf.8642$Ui.103@edtnps84>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13> <lUKNf.86659$sa3.61137@pd7tw1no> <KyZNf.8642$Ui.103@edtnps84>`

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
> news:lUKNf.86659$sa3.61137@pd7tw1no...
…[87 more quoted lines elided]…
>    - Oliver

I could foresee problems with HTML - that's why I put the final caveat 
in my last message.

The only snag with restrictions, made by a 'standards' organization like 
W3C, is that they have the possibility of inhibiting forward 
development. It makes sense W3C exists, just like J4. But human nature 
being what it is, there is the danger that standards bodies can become 
unbearably over-bureaucratic.

A classic example at the world level is the EU. I don't follow it, just 
picking up snippets from BBC World News for the most part in summarized 
form. In Brussels you have an 'artificial' parliament and it seems that 
the Eurocrats in charge are forever wanting to get member countries to 
fall into line, tout suite. There was an absurdity about a week back, 
forgotten what it was - but as an hypothetical example - you can no 
longer sell UK sausages that contain ...xxxx.

While hopefully it never gets that crass in IT - there could be limiting 
factors based on rigid standards. As a number-count, there were probably 
as many, and perhaps more, computer languages created, tried and 
dropped, prior to your 'creation', equaling the number of years you have 
had on this earth. So why did the languages come into existence at all ? 
Was it because they covered new features not part of those already 
around, or with a germ of an idea the instigator came up against a brick 
wall when trying to get a committee to go for his ideas.

Don't know whether or not you are aware, but the old hands at Xerox PARC 
are real pissed that Java outshone Smalltalk, saying it was Sun's 
marketing know-how that did the trick. True or false, it would have been 
kind of neat if instead of Java there had been a Smalltalk Version 2 
which included 'extras' plus any additional features that we see in Java 
or the 'C' group of languages. Do others like Ada have specific features 
they can add to the picture ? I realize Java was built from scratch - 
but can't help wondering if a comparison would show Smalltak and Java 
are reasonably compatible.

COBOL - Common Business Oriented Language. Think of the name and 
substitute 'Object' for 'Oriented' or make it COBOOL. Instead of going 
through the mysteries of each new language that comes along, take a 
basic brick like COBOL, which is rock solid, get rid of some of its 
verbosity without going cryptic, and attach or dovetail new features to 
it; a dreamy thought.

Then you come unstuck - couldn't do it with the same 6 members that 
COBOL J4 has. You would need something like 50 members, with very 
different interests,(machine vendors and programmers), different IT 
disciplines - communications, engineering, mathematical, 
accounting/inventory etc..  Snag is the bureaucracy would take you back 
to the EU model.

I wonder what the situation will be when the duet of China and India 
show their clout in around 2050-2070, taking over the position of Uncle 
Sam ? The PRC has shown that they aren't dumb - when the British lease 
was up on Hong Kong, Beijing merrily let Hong Kong carry on 'as is', 
because they liked the appeal of a money making machine.

So folks could pooh, pooh that thought - both counties urgently need 
oil/gas - and they are aggressively seeking sources, Arabia, Africa 
(Nigeria), S. America and up here in the Great White North, until such 
time as ethanol or hydrogen become practical alternatives. Both have 
expressed an interest in the Athabasca Tar Sands and after protracted 
negotiations will probably put up the money for a pipeline to the West 
Coast in northern B.C.

That leave us here in Alberta with a whole chunk of natural gas - one 
customer - Uncle Sam. Just not viable to consider shipping natural gas. 
However LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas), that is gas chilled down to a 
liquid level - if they want to come up with the money to finance that, 
(production facilities and piping to the coast) - then ?????

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13> <lUKNf.86659$sa3.61137@pd7tw1no> <KyZNf.8642$Ui.103@edtnps84> <jB7Of.98865$H%4.72214@pd7tw2no>`

```

In article <jB7Of.98865$H%4.72214@pd7tw2no>, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> writes:
> 
> Don't know whether or not you are aware, but the old hands at Xerox PARC 
> are real pissed that Java outshone Smalltalk, saying it was Sun's 
> marketing know-how that did the trick.

From the point of view of Xerox PARC, everyone else's marketing must
look like magic.  They're possibly the worst R&D organization ever at
conveying their ideas to the market.  There's a nice little book
titled "Fumbling the Future" which analyzes some of their most notable
failures in that area.

Personally, I think Smalltalk failed in part because it was as much a
cult as a language.  The original Smalltalk *required* the Smalltalk
GUI IDE - the language actually incorporated the IDE into its
definitions.  You couldn't just write some Smalltalk in your favorite
editor; you had to click on this piece of text with the mouse's "red
button", and on that piece with the "green button", and so on.

What's that?  Your mouse doesn't have a red button and a green button?
Well, you'll have to label your mouse buttons with the appropriate
colors before you can use Smalltalk.  You're red-green colorblind?  No
Smalltalk for you.

Later implementations, like GNU's Squeak, are apparently less militant
about this nonsense, but that was enough to turn *me* off Smalltalk
when I gave it a look.

Smalltalk was invented largely to demonstrate the superiority of the
OO concept as people at PARC envisioned it.  (Smalltalk wasn't the
first OO language - general opinion seems to be that honor goes to
Simula - but it was one of the first few.)  It was not designed to
accomodate the programmer's needs or desires.

> True or false, it would have been 
> kind of neat if instead of Java there had been a Smalltalk Version 2 
> which included 'extras' plus any additional features that we see in Java 
> or the 'C' group of languages.

Well, there was Squeak and the like, but too little, too late.

> Do others like Ada have specific features they can add to the picture ?

Sure.  Most languages have *something* of interest, as far as I've
found.  Ada has excellent safety constructs, from the little I've
seen, for example.

Java, even in its latest incarnation, lacks any number of good ideas
that are seen in other languages, like adequate support for func-
tional programming (interesting article on adding it in a recent _Dr
Dobb's_, but the result is not particularly elegant), guaranteed
timely finalization, and so forth.

The Smalltalk fans would probably suggest that Java could benefit
from Smalltalk's version of OO (rather than implementing something
closer to C++'s, albeit with only single inheritance and other key
differences).  If memory serves, Ruby and Python, for example, take
more of a Smalltalk route.

> I realize Java was built from scratch - 
> but can't help wondering if a comparison would show Smalltak and Java 
> are reasonably compatible.

Smalltalk and Java are quite different philosophically.  Of course
they're compatible in the comp-sci sense (Kolmogorov complexity and
all that), but in practice they encourage different styles of OO.
Smalltalk is all about objects requesting behavior from other
objects; the emphasis is on passing messages.  Java, like C++, sees
OO as much more about abstraction: the caller knows that a given
object is supposed to be able to do X, and it tells it to do X.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: HTML (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>

- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13> <lUKNf.86659$sa3.61137@pd7tw1no> <KyZNf.8642$Ui.103@edtnps84> <jB7Of.98865$H%4.72214@pd7tw2no> <dui1i402l64@news1.newsguy.com>`

```
Michael Wojcik wrote:
> In article <jB7Of.98865$H%4.72214@pd7tw2no>, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> writes:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> failures in that area.

Does that book point the finger at Xerox or specifically at PARC ? While
you could come up with your own oCaml derivative, you would probably 
agree that perhaps you are not the best person to actually market it, 
although you would willingly assist. That could well be why they've got 
you located in some unspecified classified location in the Midwest - 
'He's very useful, but, just in case he could be dangerous, don't let 
him get too near the rest of the M/F gang' :-)

The trouble with any marketing is that the sales boys are given the 
dictate, "Get out there and sell", coupled with forecast of dire results 
from the bean-counters, if they don't do it. Often, before the product 
is ready for sale.

Close to home, we have the minor debacle of VISOC. Nothing intrinsically 
wrong with the product, and N/E is just a series of enhancements. 
However, no Dialog System, it just offered Dialog Editor, GUI classes 
and some weird thing called Object Orientation. I only got into webbing 
just about the time VISOC was available on beta - "Come get your free 
demo copy". (They only added the postcript later - 'We'll screw you on 
pricing later' :-) ). Sat here in splendid isolation in a bedroom, I had 
never heard the term OO and had ABSOLUTELY no idea what it was about. In 
deep depression, I nearly chucked it in twice. Then I got lucky - Ed 
Arranga/Frank Coyle - Eureka !

Beg, borrow or steal a copy of their book from one of your M/F 
associates, there are bound to be copies around. Don't even need to read 
the text - just take yourself to the back of the book for one of their 
appendices - a simple library application - absolute clarity. 
Unfortunately their book back back then didn't come with source on a 
diskette or CD - so I found myself sticking pieces of paper as tabs on 
pages so I could work my way backwards and forwards between the classes 
they referenced.

Now we can't completely let the M/F developers off the hook on VISOC. 
I'm guessing they were a pretty bright bunch back then, what I would 
call the 'boffin-brigade'. (I think that guy in the Midwest might just 
fit in admirably :-) ). The classes produced were solid, and I doubt 
whether they have changed much since VISOC, other than additional 
classes added specifically for OLE, Java inter-operability etc. (Then of 
course V 4.0 adds the dotNet leg).

Those guys knew what they were doing, but as demos, the convoluted way 
they showed creation of a Master Menu from the data in the RC file. (I 
still don't understand, but it was in effect sending one character at a 
time to the Menu-Maker class). Just as confusing, look at the GUI Demo 
Phone.app and Telerec.cbl where they are Searching. The 'columns' of the 
record are interspersed with Tab pic x into which they put symbols like 
"*", "&" etc.

Perhaps you might be sympathetic to what they've done - but it sure beat 
me at the time. My approach, create a super class containing a series of 
very generic methods and you pass the column positioning to that class 
per sub-level instance. I didn't arrive at my solution in five minutes - 
but gradual progression, over a period of say three years as I 
independently became comfortable with the OO concept.

> 
> Personally, I think Smalltalk failed in part because it was as much a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Quite possibly a cult, but isn't Apple to an extent. Back in ye olden
dayse of Tandy/Radio Shack, the COLOR computer was very much a cult of
the faithful until Amiga and conventional PCs gained strength.
Don't forget you are already on record as not liking IDEs. Me, I
love'em; like Amex 'Don't leave home without it'.

Don't know where Xerox was Hq'd but possibly not near PARC. Back then I
think HQ's mindset was 'If it isn't a photo-copier, it isn't going
work'. If you can locate a VCR, 'The Pirates of Silicon Valley' - story
of Steve Job and Bill Gates, there's a scene where the PARC crowd take
their ideas to the main board. The CEO or chairman, with a supercilious
smile says, "And what do you call this er, thing ? A mouse ?"

> What's that?  Your mouse doesn't have a red button and a green button?
> Well, you'll have to label your mouse buttons with the appropriate
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Agreed. I mentioned Smalltalk here quite a few years ago. In jumps
somebody from Norway with a site reference. I think there may be a few
who still use Simula. Referencing Smalltalk, they acknowledge Simula's
roots. What led me personally to Smalltalk was Net Express. Very initial
and primitive demo of using collections. The reply I received, "N/E
OO Collections were based on Smalltalk"; so is Hitachi's original model. 
Went looking and found Adele Goldberg's book on Smalltalk 80. It isn't 
what I would categorize as a 'Teach yourself Smalltalk', lots of
narrative with some explained examples. Pleasantly found that the
Collection structure of Smalltalk and N/E were almost identical - the 
latter even using many of the the same method names.

> 
>>True or false, it would have been 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Well, there was Squeak and the like, but too little, too late.

Back when, went googling and found something called Squeak. You've got 
to be kidding ! Looked at the demo dialog which used all (16 ?) prime 
set of colours - dead ugly and amateurish - not the crisp Apple look 
that Steve moved to when he pinched their ideas. (I've never touched an 
'Apple', just seen pictures). Funnily enough, googling a couple of days 
ago I picked up on Squeak again. Took a quick look - appears to have 
been enhanced from what I previously saw. Whether a cult or not, there's 
the commercial version 'Dolphin' which has its own Newsgroup. Looks 
fairly active to me.
> 
> 
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Philosophy, a very good point, and extremely important - still no 
different from you and me with our respective approaches perhaps to how 
a compiler should be constructed, or two developers producing Accounts 
Receivable in their own style, even when both work from the same Project 
Request specification.

Jimmy
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13> <lUKNf.86659$sa3.61137@pd7tw1no> <KyZNf.8642$Ui.103@edtnps84> <jB7Of.98865$H%4.72214@pd7tw2no> <dui1i402l64@news1.newsguy.com> <dQ1Qf.128480$H%4.38190@pd7tw2no>`

```

In article <dQ1Qf.128480$H%4.38190@pd7tw2no>, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> writes:
> Michael Wojcik wrote:
> > In article <jB7Of.98865$H%4.72214@pd7tw2no>, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> writes:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does that book point the finger at Xerox or specifically at PARC ?

I don't recall specifics - it's been many years since I read it - but
both probably deserve some of the blame, though the parent company
gets the larger share.  PARC, from everything I've read and seen,
rarely did a very good job of promoting the results of its R&D.  I
think IBM, for example, has done much better over the years, and of
course recently Microsoft among others has really done an amazing job
of drumming up interest in new tech.

But whenever I've read interviews with (ex-)PARC people (they used to
show up periodically in publications like _Wired_), they came across
as a combination of ivory-tower dreamers and wild-eyed believers.
And I say this as a academic, at heart, who's quite fond of ivory
towers in many circumstances.

The sense I get from those PARC interviews and publications of yore
is not "here's an idea we think is clever and perhaps useful to you",
but "here's an idea which is clearly stunningly brilliant and you
ought to immediately adopt it without reservation".  And that's a
trifle off-putting, particularly when the parent company is so good
at subsequently dropping the ball - so I feel I ought to hesitate
before committing to anything coming out of PARC for practical
reasons.

Take, for example, this Alan Kay quote I pinched from the Squeak
website (www.squeak.org):

   The real romance is out ahead and yet to come. The computer
   revolution hasn't started yet. Don't be misled by the enormous
   flow of money into bad defacto standards for unsophisticated
   buyers using poor adaptations of incomplete ideas.

Ah, Alan, you silver-tongued devil.  Your flattery has convinced me!
You're the visionary, we're the proles.

Putting the boot in *is* one way to run a revolution, but it's not
always the most effective way.

> While
> you could come up with your own oCaml derivative, you would probably 
> agree that perhaps you are not the best person to actually market it, 
> although you would willingly assist.

This is true.  I'm not temperamentally suited to selling - and the
same can probably be said of many of the folks who worked at PARC.

> That could well be why they've got 
> you located in some unspecified classified location in the Midwest - 
> 'He's very useful, but, just in case he could be dangerous, don't let 
> him get too near the rest of the M/F gang' :-)

Heh.  I suppose that explains why they don't let me single-handedly
set product direction...

> Close to home, we have the minor debacle of VISOC. Nothing intrinsically 
> wrong with the product, and N/E is just a series of enhancements. 
> However, no Dialog System, it just offered Dialog Editor, GUI classes 
> and some weird thing called Object Orientation.

I must admit I never did anything significant with VISOC.  At the
time I was working on an unrelated product (AAI, one of our
middleware offerings, which supported but did not use COBOL); by
the time I got responsibility for some COBOL components, we were
well into the Net Express era.

But I take your point - VISOC had too much future and not enough past
in it, which is a real problem.

> ... Then I got lucky - Ed Arranga/Frank Coyle - Eureka !
> 
> Beg, borrow or steal a copy of their book from one of your M/F 
> associates, there are bound to be copies around.

Next time I'm at one of the big offices I'll ask around.  I have
Ray Obin's OO COBOL book.  I need to spend some more time with OO
COBOL for a little side research project I'm fooling with on my
own time anyway.

> Now we can't completely let the M/F developers off the hook on VISOC. 
> I'm guessing they were a pretty bright bunch back then, what I would 
> call the 'boffin-brigade'.

Still pretty boffiny around here; MF seems to cater to the type
that likes to get their hands dirty.

> (I think that guy in the Midwest might just fit in admirably :-) ).

You are too kind, but thanks.

> > Personally, I think Smalltalk failed in part because it was as much a
> > cult as a language.  The original Smalltalk *required* the Smalltalk
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Quite possibly a cult, but isn't Apple to an extent.

Well, yes, but I'm not really an Apple fan, either.  I don't like
black boxes (comes from my boffin inclinations I suppose).  Of
course, with OS X, once you know a couple of key points like how
to open a Terminal window, how to boot in single-user mode, etc
you can get under the hood, so to speak.  That's been a great help
every time I've had to fix something on one of the various Macs
around me.  (My wife and stepdaughter are both Mac users, as are
most of the people who work for my wife.)

> Back in ye olden
> dayse of Tandy/Radio Shack, the COLOR computer was very much a cult of
> the faithful until Amiga and conventional PCs gained strength.

Yes, and there were many fans of the Apple II, and the various
pre-Amiga Commodore and Atari machines, etc.  I did quite a bit
of tinkering on various 8- and 16-bit home boxes.

> Don't forget you are already on record as not liking IDEs. Me, I
> love'em; like Amex 'Don't leave home without it'.

Sure, but isn't it nice that with both languages we can each satisfy
our preferences?  Though I admit that what really got me with the
Smalltalk IDE was the colored-button business.  I have poor color
vision to begin with, and Sun had inexplicably failed to mark the
Smalltalk colors on my mouse buttons.  Argh!

The language design itself I quite like in some respects.  The "pure"
message-passing OO model, the use of Model-View-Controller pattern,
and so on was very nice for certain kinds of applications.

> Back then I
> think HQ's mindset was 'If it isn't a photo-copier, it isn't going
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> smile says, "And what do you call this er, thing ? A mouse ?"

I've seen, and enjoyed, it, and while I don't recall that particular
scene it rings true.

> >>True or false, it would have been 
> >>kind of neat if instead of Java there had been a Smalltalk Version 2 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> that Steve moved to when he pinched their ideas.

Apparently Squeak has gotten better over the years - the self-styled
SqueakFoundation is pushing it as a commercially viable general-
purpose language, with all sorts of libraries and whatnot.  It was
originally suggested to me by various Smalltalk proponents because it
had a purely-text source code mode, unlike the original Smalltalk.

> Funnily enough, googling a couple of days 
> ago I picked up on Squeak again. Took a quick look - appears to have 
> been enhanced from what I previously saw. Whether a cult or not, there's 
> the commercial version 'Dolphin' which has its own Newsgroup. Looks 
> fairly active to me.

Yes, but will it ever become a major contender - even in the second
tier, like Python and perhaps Ruby?  I wouldn't mind seeing that
happen (I'm all for software development moving toward more
expressive and safer languages, and variety is good), but there are
so many viable programming languages today that it's tough even for
the really interesting ones to be heard in the clamour.

> >>I realize Java was built from scratch - 
> >>but can't help wondering if a comparison would show Smalltak and Java 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Request specification.

Sure.  I just meant that it's tough to take a non-trivial program
in idiomatic Java and convert it to idiomatic Smalltalk, or vice
versa.  You can do the same things in both languages, of course,
but you wouldn't necessarily want to do them in the same way.  That
application might best be designed quite differently in the two
languages.
```

- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13>`

```

"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
news:IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
First off, Oliver, I ALWAYS read your posts. :-)

Secondly, although I may have issues with COPBOL standards, the W3C is 
osmething else again. I am interested in your post.

>    Bad HTML versus good HTML might be subjective, but I dislike invalid 
> (non-standard conforming) HTML.
>

I think that would be the consensus from most of us who spend time doing web 
pages. I know that a little while back, Daniel helped sort out some 
non-standard code I had, and so did Richard and others. They first drew my 
attention to the point you are making. I had checked the site with 
Dreamweaver and it reported it was OK for Netscape (I knew it was fine for 
IE), but it wasn't. To be fair, this was a pretty old edition of Dreamweaver 
and I have recently replaced it with the latest Dreamweaver in Studio 8 
which seems to do a much better job. These days I check pages I do in 
Firefox, as well as getting DW to check for various Browsers.

> From http://www.webstandards.org/about/
> <quote>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That last paragraph was a very real dilemma for me. Having spent almost 1000 
hours developing and meticulously checking in IE that everything worked how 
it should, it was a major problem to then find that it simply didn't work in 
Netscape (for example...)

>    It is not enough for the website to simply "look okay" on any one 
> particular browser; the HTML should be structurally correct, and the tags 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I agree and don't do this. But it goes way beyond that. Table renditions, 
JavaScript, ASP code, can all render differentloy in different Browsers. It 
is a problem. It is tempting to just decide to develop for IE (line of least 
resistance and over 90% of hits on my host's web server), but that does 
leave a bad taste, even for someone like mself who has no axe to grind with 
MS.

>    For example, perhaps you have a style guide which says quotation 
> attributions should be displayed in italics. You should note write the 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hmmmm.... here we may part company. :-)

While I have every sympathy for Mr Gumson's affliction, I think this is 
litigation gone mad. If he was unable to make a reservation on their web 
site then that, in my opinion is simply bad luck. What if the server was 
down? Should everybody who couldn't make a booking sue the airline? What 
about people who are "disabled" by being unable to read? Or everyone who 
doesn't have internet access? Or people who only speak Swahili or Hottentot?

My point is that when you build a web site you don't guarantee that the 
entire planet can access it (even though you hope they will, and this drives 
you to make it as open as possible.)

Mr. Gumson could have used a telephone or some other channel to obtain his 
reservation. Only in America  (where litigation is a multi-billion dollar 
industry) would someone think of suing, for something that is not an 
intended discrimination. You use a web site at your own risk. Even if you 
CAN read it, it may contain false information...

You might as well sue the milkman because he has no low fat milk this 
morning. There is no contract that says he must have, any more than there is 
a contract that says the airline's web site must be usable by everyone.

While I sympathise with web standards that try to get as much as possible 
working across as many platforms as possible, I don't think there is cause 
for litigation if it falls down. (If the web was the ONLY channel and it was 
a matter of life or death, there might be, but under those circumstances, it 
would be a very brave man indeed who would build the web site...)

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13> <46qr1vFcgpnoU1@individual.net>`

```
I have deleted all the old parts of this thread, but did think I would put in my 
"two cents" worth on web pages and disabilities.  (I don't know if Pete is aware 
that American Airlines, and several other US-based air-carriers are currently 
adding a "surcharge" for reservations made by phone and not via their web-site. 
However, I am not certain that I think that this warrants a law-suite because 
some customers have "problems" with a website).

Relatively recently I was on my villages (about 30,000 population) "commission 
for persons with disabilities".  During that time I did some research into the 
recommendations for websites (and as far as some government bodies the 
REQUIREMENT for) "user friendly for ALL persons with disabilities".  It was 
interesting research into various topics such as why and how to provide 
"color-independent" emphasis, graphics-free information, etc.

I looked at some of the "reference" (or example sites) and found them pretty 
UN-interesting and could well imagine that commercial vendors would find the 
restrictions counter-productive.

On the other hand, as a person with a visual impairment, I also appreciate when 
sites DO allow me to control font size and other features to make reading their 
sites easier.

If anyone in this site is interested (and doesn't already have such information 
available), I could dig up some of the sites with recommendations on how to 
build (what to do and not do) a site that "disabled-friendly".

P.S.  I should add that I know my sites (such as the COBOL FAQ) do NOT meet all 
the recommendations; just as I know that my use of Microsoft software to produce 
HTML is not Browser indecent (in many/some cases).  If I charged for my sites or 
expected to do "business" based on what I post, I would certainly feel an 
obligation to IMPROVE how I develop HTML.  However, for my "freebie" 
informational sites, I usually try and document my restrictions (and ask for 
input on problems) and just "go with the flow". <G>
```

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <cfmtech@istar.ca>

- **References:** `<dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <IPDNf.11717$dg.1827@clgrps13> <46qr1vFcgpnoU1@individual.net> <iSXNf.184512$H54.175366@fe03.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Fri, 03 Mar 2006 13:52:14 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I have deleted all the old parts of this thread, but did think I would put in my 
>"two cents" worth on web pages and disabilities.  (I don't know if Pete is aware 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>some customers have "problems" with a website).

There are US laws and standards regarding web sites and accessibility
to those who have various physical limitations (to meet them a site
has to be able to handle all of the listed disabilities but is not
required to handle all combinations of disabilities).  These include
such things as not having the ability to use a mouse.  I heard the
person from IBM who played a part in making sure the US standards and
international standards were the same.  It was then embarrassing when
another group within IBM was pitching a product to the Social Security
Administration and didn't have a clue about the standards.  In this
case, Southwest as a public carrier is probably required by law to
have its site accessible and a lawsuit probably will be successful.
>
>Relatively recently I was on my villages (about 30,000 population) "commission 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>input on problems) and just "go with the flow". <G>
```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:46qr1vFcgpnoU1@individual.net...
>
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> site...)

    Yes, I provided that quote without context. I didn't mean to say one 
side is "morally right" in this lawsuit; rather, I meant to say "not 
following standards can cause some very real problems for certain people".

    I also haven't personally verified this myself, but from what I heard 
(from comments on blogs, etc.) is that in America, all businesses with a 
"physical presence" must meet certain disability guidelines, of which the 
guidelines from ADA (http://www.usdoj.gov/crt/ada/adahom1.htm) are the ones 
which apply to websites; and that when you sue a company for violating the 
ADA, you are not allowed to sue for money, only to sue to force compliance.

    - Oliver

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HTML (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-04T23:03:28+13:00

I have a particular interest in visual impairment (and have developed a
site for a local charity dealing with v.i. issues) and noted, in
researching visual disablement that a solution which works for one
impaired individual may manage to make matters worse for a dissimilarly
impaired individual.

>
> My point is that when you build a web site you don't guarantee that the
> entire planet can access it (even though you hope they will, and this drives
> you to make it as open as possible.)

Unfortunately, both US and UK law expect you to make your site usuable
by visually and physically impaired individuals.

>

Litigation. I have not checked to see what the penalties are likely to
be (pecuniary I presume). There are possible defences in the notions of
browser compatibility (audio browsers tend to lag behind others),
mutually incompatible solutions for various disabilities, having made
demonstrable effort to reduce the impact of impairment through the web
site. I expect there are others. However, making no effort is no
excuse. BTW, British national and local government web sites are major
culprits in disability issues.

- **In-Reply-To:** `<46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> In addition to new methodologies which departed from the procedural model, 
> hardware was making even more dramatic leaps and bounds. Things got smaller, 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> for, and is not good at, this.

The more things change, the more they stay the same.  Have you heard of 
"diskless workstations" and the Linux Terminal Server Project?  They're 
being used as dumb terminals (minimum system requirement - original 
Pentium 133, 32MB RAM, 2MB video memory).  They boot from the network, 
and they only thing they do is render video from X (GUI) sessions off 
the server.

I'm planning on trying a setup like this at my children's school, in 
their library.  Set 'em up with Firefox to search the library's 
catalog(ue), and *poof*!  Instead of 10 useless computers, they now have 
10 prime card catalog(ue) lookup stations!

http://www.ltsp.org is the website...  It makes a lot of sense.  Users 
can connect from any of the workstations, and they'll mount the same 
home directory - their files (and security profiles) follow them.

I'm sure I'll be even more fired up about it once I actually get it to 
work.  :)  (I'm also going to try to set something up like that here, so 
I can utilize the old computers I thought were just going to be tossed 
in the trash.)

Who'd have thought - a mainframe with GUI clients!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF having issues?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-04T02:50:37+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46qvtgFc9rc9U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <3533a$4407b31a$45491d7a$20406@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:3533a$4407b31a$45491d7a$20406@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> In addition to new methodologies which departed from the procedural 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> "diskless workstations" and the Linux Terminal Server Project?

I know about diskless workstations and considered it for an application in 
England a couple of years back. I didn't know about the LTSP, but I have 
very little exposure to Linux.

 They're
> being used as dumb terminals (minimum system requirement - original 
> Pentium 133, 32MB RAM, 2MB video memory).  They boot from the network, and 
> they only thing they do is render video from X (GUI) sessions off the 
> server.
>

Hmmm... not 'thin', rather, 'emaciated' clients :-)

Distribution and Centralization have alternated in waves since the late 70s 
when it first became possible to distribute. I remember the Cyber 70 series 
which had separate processors to do its I/O so the CPU could concentrate on 
code execution. CDC were very proud of this 'distributed' architecture.

While it is certainly feasible to do this kind of thing, I believe that 
there is still a place for 'local intelligence' when it comes to utilizing 
bandwidth in the most effective way. Your diskless workstations will require 
constant refresh from the server (rather than caching it themselves) and 
all editing and validation will be done at the backend, which simply means 
error notifications and corrections must also be pushed down the line. A lot 
depends on what's driving it, the bandwidth, and the protocols. (Field by 
field, full screen, lots of different factors here that can influence 
performance.)

For simply serving up information, this could be a very good solution. I 
have some reservations where volume data entry is occurring, but I haven't 
looked inot deeply enough to have an opinion as yet.

I think there is a place for centralization for certain types of application 
(and yours may be one), but I don't see it as a general solution.

> I'm planning on trying a setup like this at my children's school, in their 
> library.  Set 'em up with Firefox to search the library's catalog(ue), and 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> the trash.)

It certainly sounds like fun. :-)

Good luck!

>
> Who'd have thought - a mainframe with GUI clients!
>
If it wasn't for that fat penguin,  I'd go and look at it :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF having issues?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-03T17:01:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1141434099.274392.40070@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<46qvtgFc9rc9U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <3533a$4407b31a$45491d7a$20406@KNOLOGY.NET> <46qvtgFc9rc9U1@individual.net>`

```

Pete Dashwood wrote:
>  They're
> > being used as dumb terminals (minimum system requirement - original
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hmmm... not 'thin', rather, 'emaciated' clients :-)

I use LTSP on my client's sites. The old PCs that would otherwise be
landfill with all the disks ripped out and a PXE ROM in the NIC
(translation: boots over network) makes a reliable terminal. It boots
over the net and then attaches using NFS back to the server. Logins use
the user's home directory on the server so logging in from anywhere
gives the same setup and file set for the user. The terminal only runs
the X server (plus it can load and run selected software as
appropriate), the applications run on the server (or another server)
and therefore use the server's disk and CPU power.

> While it is certainly feasible to do this kind of thing, I believe that
> there is still a place for 'local intelligence' when it comes to utilizing
> bandwidth in the most effective way. Your diskless workstations will require
> constant refresh from the server (rather than caching it themselves) and

If it was Windows then it would require 'constant refresh from the
server', but this is X and this was designed from the start to be a
multiuser/multiserver network protocol. So no, it does not require
'refresh' at all and does local caching and does use bandwidth
effectively.

> all editing and validation will be done at the backend, which simply means
> error notifications and corrections must also be pushed down the line. A lot
> depends on what's driving it, the bandwidth, and the protocols. (Field by
> field, full screen, lots of different factors here that can influence
> performance.)

<shrug> the server runs the database engine so a fat client will be
sucking database accesses over the network. Which uses more bandwith
depends on what gets sent over the network. X maintains the graphics
screen in the client so only 'api calls' go between the application and
the X server (the client machine GUI engine is the server for X, the
application program is the client).

> For simply serving up information, this could be a very good solution. I
> have some reservations where volume data entry is occurring, but I haven't
> looked inot deeply enough to have an opinion as yet.

Having the application on the same box as the isam files/database
engine is __FAR__ faster than shovelling file data over the network.
Don't mix this up with Windows TSE which sucks bandwidth big time.

> I think there is a place for centralization for certain types of application
> (and yours may be one), but I don't see it as a general solution.

There is usually some level of centralization, most often the minimum
is the database.
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<46qvtgFc9rc9U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <3533a$4407b31a$45491d7a$20406@KNOLOGY.NET> <46qvtgFc9rc9U1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:3533a$4407b31a$45491d7a$20406@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hmmm... not 'thin', rather, 'emaciated' clients :-)

Yep - somewhere between "thin" and "dumb"...  (Can't think of a good 
word, but the name "Calista" keeps coming to mind...)

> While it is certainly feasible to do this kind of thing, I believe that 
> there is still a place for 'local intelligence' when it comes to utilizing 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> performance.)

I've yet to see it in action, but supposedly, this isn't the way it's 
designed.  The "X" server (the beefy Linux box) communicates with the 
"X" client (which runs in RAM on the workstation) - so, the only thing 
that would be passed across the network is the internal stuff you'd 
normally have between the OS and its GUI layer.  (Of course, streaming 
video is another matter... :>  But, some folks have run 40-50 clients on 
a 100-mbps network with no noticeable problems.

I guess that's the difference between it and something like VNC.  VNC is 
rendering an exact copy of something on another display.  The LTSP 
clients are actually rendering the GUI themselves - so, I imagine things 
like changing focus when you tab to a new field would be done by the 
client, not the server.  (Any onchange events, though, would fire on the 
server - that's the way it's supposed to work, though.)

> For simply serving up information, this could be a very good solution. I 
> have some reservations where volume data entry is occurring, but I haven't 
> looked inot deeply enough to have an opinion as yet.

My interest is in a library environment, where users would be able to 
browse the net, as well as do things like working on documents, from any 
of the workstations.  Administration would be a breeze, and the users 
could actually save files on the server, and get to them no matter where 
they logged on.

>> Who'd have thought - a mainframe with GUI clients!
>>
> If it wasn't for that fat penguin,  I'd go and look at it :-)

Tux being fat is a good thing!  It means that he's always at the 
keyboard, making things better!  ;)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF having issues?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-06T14:33:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1141684416.504076.277550@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<2115e$440b9740$45491d7a$2155@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <46o0i6FbssfnU1@individual.net> <3533a$4407b31a$45491d7a$20406@KNOLOGY.NET> <46qvtgFc9rc9U1@individual.net> <2115e$440b9740$45491d7a$2155@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

LX-i wrote:
> I've yet to see it in action, but supposedly, this isn't the way it's
> designed.  The "X" server (the beefy Linux box) communicates with the
> "X" client (which runs in RAM on the workstation) -

In X terminology they are the other way around.  The X Server runs on
the client machine or terminal and controls the actual display, the X
Clients are the application programs that are displaying data and
getting input from the server.

This is reasonable as for each server (screen, keyboard, mouse) there
may be several client programs, which may be distributed around the
network.  For example I can start a program on my archive machine in
the garage and have it connect to my X Server running a KDE session
locally and it will open a window as if it were just another program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF having issues?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-03T18:44:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46s9oqFcgn9iU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <1141248351.375659.50400@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com>`

```
<cblkid@yahoo.com> 03/01/06 2:25 PM >>>
>I'd like to Thank everyone who replied. From what I've seen and heard
>this week it doesn't look like this 'stumble' will have that much of an
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>know why, guess I like fighting for the 'perceived' underdog).

Hmm, I'd guess we have maybe 1500 COBOL programs.  And I certainly wouldn't
consider replacing them with any packaged programs.  Nor would I rewrite
most of them just for the heck of it, even if we had an alternative to COBOL
(which we don't).  But if we did have an alternative to COBOL (and honestly,
I don't mean Java!) that was "better" than COBOL (better, of course, being
my opinion only), I would certainly love to see new development in this new
language, and perhaps some rewriting of old programs if necessary to
interface to these new programs.

But there certainly is the issue of not COBOL being a *bad* language, just
that it's not a *popular* one, at least for the "younger" generation (I'm
only 36, so...).  We had a heck of a time finding a single new COBOL
developer last year.  Not that there weren't a lot of applications (I'm not
sure), but there just weren't a lot of the kind of person we were looking
for.  Is that the fault of COBOL?  Maybe...

Personally, I'm just trying to "live" with COBOL.  Just today I was working
on taking Michael Wojcik's suggestion with regard to using some OO features
in non-OO COBOL.  So far it is looking promising; but it's only been one
day...

Frank


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF having issues?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-03-04T02:09:28+00:00

```
In article <46s9oqFcgn9iU1@individual.net>,
Frank Swarbrick <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:

[snip]

>We had a heck of a time finding a single new COBOL
>developer last year.  Not that there weren't a lot of applications (I'm not
>sure), but there just weren't a lot of the kind of person we were looking
>for.  Is that the fault of COBOL?  Maybe...

With all due respect, Mr Swarbrick, I fail to see how someone's not being 
'the kind of person we were looking for' is the fault of any 
computer-programming language at all.

(Wanted: twenty-five years' worth of experience at two-to-five years' 
salary.  No relocation expenses paid and your asking about a 'career path' 
will get you shown the door.)

DD
```

>will get you shown the door.)

I simply meant that since "COBOL isn't cool" there are less people inclined
to learn it, and thus a smaller pool of programmers to choose from.  It's
not the fault of the language in and of itself, but it's perhaps the fault
of the reputation that the language has.

Frank

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF having issues?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-03-07T01:57:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<duip9l$m12$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <46s9oqFcgn9iU1@individual.net> <duasso$sfi$1@reader2.panix.com> <4743tsFd96qvU2@individual.net>`

```
In article <4743tsFd96qvU2@individual.net>,
Frank Swarbrick <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> 03/03/06 7:09 PM >>>
>>In article <46s9oqFcgn9iU1@individual.net>,
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>of the reputation that the language has.

It might be, Mr Swarbrick, that - in the slang of those who used to 
consume tobacco-producte - if tailor-mades aren't available one might do 
well to roll one's own.  From 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/6c29c3f3dc17173c?dmode=source&hl=en>

--begin quoted text:

Years ago the Wall Street Journal did a story on one of the major NY
houses... I think it was Morgan Stanley or Morgan Guaranty or the like. 
They hired *only* the 'unhireable'... kids with BAs in Library Science,
Art History, etc... they put these kids through two years of hell, 60 -
70 hr weeks, and turned them into *crackerjack* programmers... and then
saw said kids being hired away by the competition at double or triple
the salary.  When asked why a raise did not accompany the completion of
the course the HR representative replied 'Oh, we cannot do that... all
the money has been taken up by training.' (compare this with 'Oh, we
cannot upgrade the door-locks... all the money went into oil-paintings
to hang on the walls.')

--end quoted text

So... 'the kind of people' might be out there, but they might need stimuli 
like training and bonuses and being made to that they are important to the 
organisation... you know, stuff that often gets reserved for the Managers, 
not mere (sniff) technical folks.

In your defense, Mr Swarbrick, I recall that you work for a bank... and 
banks are, in my experience, *notoriously* foolish when it comes to such 
things.  I can't recall the details of the project but I remember reading 
a Computerworld article how a VP of DP at Mellon Bank in Pittsburgh wanted 
to use one of those newfangled IBM PC AT machines for something... and had 
to go through five other levels of management to get sign-off on a 
US$20,000 project... that wound up saving the office US$60,000 in its 
first year of implementation.

DD
```

path'
>>>will get you shown the door.)
>>

inclined

fault
>>of the reputation that the language has.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
><http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/6c29c3f3dc17173c?dmode=
source&hl=en>
>
>--begin quoted text:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>So... 'the kind of people' might be out there, but they might need stimuli

>like training and bonuses and being made to that they are important to the

>organisation... you know, stuff that often gets reserved for the Managers,

>not mere (sniff) technical folks.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>a Computerworld article how a VP of DP at Mellon Bank in Pittsburgh wanted

>to use one of those newfangled IBM PC AT machines for something... and had

>to go through five other levels of management to get sign-off on a 
>US$20,000 project... that wound up saving the office US$60,000 in its 
>first year of implementation.

As it happens, quite a few of our programmers are employees that started in
other areas of the bank and then later moved in to programming.  Of the 19
COBOL programmers on staff, six worked for the bank in other areas prior to
moving to the programming department.  I myself am one of those six.  We
have a new trainee starting within the next month or two who currently works
in another department.  (He has to finish some projects there before he
transfers up).

In the case I refer to above we were specifically looking for a more senior
COBOL programmer, so obviously an inhouse employee was out of the question.

As for being conservative, I'm sure we are somewhat, but certainly not to
the extent of your example above.

Frank


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA

Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> As it happens, quite a few of our programmers are employees that started in
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

Frank,

To my mind, a very enlightened organization. Sure I enjoyed my period as 
a bean-counter before getting into systems analysis, but was more than 
delighted to make the change.

The implication is that your in-house recruits, including yourself, were 
of a 'curious' mindset, When, What, Why, How etc., which led you to DP 
when the chance came. And just as importantly you brought knowledge of 
the real world in which the bank's applications would be used.

Yes, I realize that inevitably you can recruit from outside - but was 
curious based on what you wrote. 19 people, varying lengths of time in 
COBOL, and if I recall you said you yourself had 10 years COBOL under 
your belt. Unless you were after somebody with a cracker-jack expertise 
with a specific tool - nobody from the bank could have slotted into the 
spot ?

Jimmy

in credit/debit cards) experience.  And that's what we got!  Just took a
while.  We could, and we are going to, train some of our current programmers
on the system, but we also wanted to get in someone who had a head start
from having already dealt with similar systems.  Plus we just needed another
programmer, period.

Frank
