```
Oliver Wong wrote:

Oliver,

COBOL Collections - so far as COBOL is concerned it's a little 
complicated. Firstly Fujitsu, Hitachi and Micro Focus independently 
produced their own collection 'styles', while J4 had a dummy-run (in 
'96) going from memory, which was put on ice until Bill Klein 
resurrected the topic - I know fully well his buddies on J4 at the time, 
*absolutely* loved him for doing that.

So there is a TR for Collections, again don't know where they are at, as 
I know longer look. However going from memory :-

OrderedCollection
KeyedCollection ( which I identify as Dictionary)
SortedCollection
IteratorClass (known to me in M/F as Callback )
Collection Exceptions

Now the current M/F Collection Hierarchy is, and I wont attempt to 
indent, because that is just plain tedious :-

Collection
Bag
sequencedcollection
ArrayedCollection
Array
ByteArray
CharacterArray
UnicodeString
EventArray
dynamicArrayedCollection
orderedcollection
sortedcollection
ValueSet
dictionary
identitydictionary
SymbolTable
IdentitySet
DBCScharacterarray
entryCallback
Callback
ThreadedCallback

The above is based on Smalltalk. Starting at Collection the shown 
hierarchy inherits from Dependent which inherits from Base. Above is not 
totally exhaustive as M/F also has Association which is used to build 
Dictionaries, plus classes covering COBOL PICX and COMP5 etc.

Your familiarity with what is available in Java, no doubt is immediately 
raising a question as to the disparity between the above two. I tried to 
tell 'em. One more nail in the coffin - what's currently available in 
F/J and M/F, and what is intended means there will never be any 
portability between PC-compilers, nor a snow's chance in hell of 
swapping code.

As an example my usage is SortedCollection (80% plus), sometimes 
OrderedCollection and Dictionary. Possibly the most frequently used is 
CharacterArray used to turn text into displayable objects and vice 
versa, for GUI-ing. But whichever of the 'common' classes I use, they 
are constantly zipping backwards and forwards to many of the others for 
support.

Now to quickly answer some of the observations you made :-

> The way it works is when you create a new ArrayList, you can specify its 
> initial capacity. If you don't, it just uses some arbitrary 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> capacity exactly matches its current size to save a bit of memory.

Slightly different wording to yours, but that's what I can get. Your 
comment about a default size of 10 - same thing with M/F
> 
>    Not sure if I understood the question, but if you want a dictionary 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> dictionary.

Can't have a duplicate Key in a collection anymore than you can have a 
duplicate key in a COBOL file. Try to add an element creating a 
duplicate key - it just gets ignored. I can have :

Key---> then Data
-----------------
12345 Micro Focus Ltd
12347 Micro Focus Ltd

but I can't have :

Micro Focus Ltd	12345
Micro Focus Ltd 12347

Wasn't too clear about your reference to Pairs - but the following 
example from Adele Goldberg (Smalltalk 80) is allowable to find 
'Opposites' from the Key :-

Key---> then Data
-----------------
hot	cold
push	pull
stop	go
come	go
front	back
top	bottom

I think 'Pairs' you are possibly referring to something slightly different ?

>    I don't know if the library you're using supports this, but droplists 
> in Java can have an "ListCellRenderer" object associated with them. The 
> ListCellRenderer object takes the actual content of the list, and takes 
> care of their appearances on the GUI.

That was fun - ran the demo you recommended. Basically its a dropdown 
plus an area for a bitmap - n'est-il pas ? Haven't got into webbing but 
M/F does have features for URL-ing, so design the dialog to take the 
piggy, cat, or dog bitmap - in theory should work. But I'm only likely 
to take a crack at it in Java.

Lots of good support in GUI classes but not totally. Gets tough say 
colouring a dropdown - you need from memory WmDrawItem, so that for each 
  element, you draw the text area then colour it. Associated with that I 
require properties OwnerDraw and HasStrings - the only way I can get the 
latter is to visually check-off those two properties when using the 
visual Dialog Editor. For dynamic use there just aren't methods for 
"OwnerDraw" and "HasStrings" !

Thanks for the interesting info - Java gets more and more appealing 
unless somebody really plugs C. I haven't heard back  from the two 
COBOLers I know are switching to C#. My suspicion 'We'll go with C# 'cos 
we think we are safer with Bill Gates.  Now as to what trick might be 
pulled in Winders if we go Java....?"  Just a guess, but that might be 
their logic.

Jimmy
```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:uJNPf.128728$B94.114109@pd7tw3no...
>>
>>    Not sure if I understood the question, but if you want a dictionary 
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> ?

    Yes, I meant something different.

    In Java as well, you can't have a dictionary (though it's called a "Map" 
in Java) where two objects have the same key. I was saying you could "fake" 
it by having a "Pair" object whose sole purpose is to contain references to 
the two objects you *do* want to store.

Key --> Data
123    (Hot, Cold)
124    (push, pull)
125    (stop, go)
etc.

The idea is that only one object, the "pair" object, is associate with a 
given key. But the pair object is like a collection whose size is guaranteed 
to always be 2. Obviously, you can extend this idea if you wanted to 
simulate 3 objects per key:

Key --> Data
123   (Hot, Warm, Cold)
124   (push, rest, pull)
125   (red, green, blue)
etc.

    - Oliver
```

Oliver Wong wrote:

Hey I have looked at rudimentaries - I knew Map = Dictionary :-)

Now I understand your 'Pairs' concept, your examples of 'doubles', 
'triples' etc., that would work in the M/F dictionary class; and the J4 
proposed KeyedCollection.

Mind you - if you think about it, the most logical thing is to have 
names/descriptors as Dictionary Keys. I can't see you get much mileage 
asking a dictionary "Do you have an element with a Key '12345' ?
There are several methods which pick up on the data, but I have never 
used them.

One possibility -  use a callback/iterator against the whole collection 
automatically checking each element. Then terminate with 
'quitIteration', when you have found what you want.

My prime interest was converting data files written in RM/COBOL to 
either M/F Files or SQL Tables. As part of 'normalization' I wanted to 
get rid of duplicate descriptors stored in 700 data sets, (very small 
volumes each, 20 - 30 entries). Example :

Gas Plant A - System 123 - Gas Scrubber
Gas Plant B - System 456 - Gas Scrubber

Using mnemonic codes in the DB Table, GS = Gas Scrubber - build 
Dictionary from existing codes in the DB Table and as I read in each RM 
entry do a look-up : (1) If found use new "GS" code, or (2) add a new 
code to the Dictionary and write it to the DB Table - the M/F 
files/Tables written for Plants A and B above contain the new mnemonics.

TOP POST - no more

Jimmy
>

…[65 more quoted lines elided]…

```
Anyone for "associate arrays"?

In the language D you can do the following:

int[char[]] b;		// associative array b of ints that are
			// indexed by an array of characters.
			// The KeyType is char[]
b["hello"] = 3;		// set value associated with key "hello" to 3
func(b["hello"]);	// pass 3 as parameter to func()

This is from http://www.digitalmars.com/d/arrays.html#associative if you're
interested.  What I like about it is it's directly supported by the
language.

So for Oliver's example, I'd imagine something like:

class Pair {
  DisplayableObject displayable;
  TextForMethodName text;
}

Pair[char[]] p;
p["key"] = new Pair();
p["key"].displayable = myDisplayableObject;
p["key"].text = "Object Label";

print(p["anotherKey"].text);

or possibly:

Pair[char[]] pairs;
Pair p = new Pair();
p.displayable = myDisplayableObject;
p.text = "Object Label";
pairs["key"] = p;

print(pairs["anotherKey"].text);

Don't have the D compiler at work, so I can't check it, but it looks about
right...

Frank


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

<snip>

If you look at the listing I gave Oliver of the M/F Collection 
hierarchy, you will see references to classes on Arrays. No idea if 
people use them much. (Yes, they are used indirectly to support 'true' 
Collection classes). But the important factor is your, "What I like 
about it is it's directly supported by the language".

Perhaps it can be achieved using M/F methods - but if you look at the 
proposed Class Structure for Collections drafted by J4 (Standards) - you 
are SOL. Just don't hold your breath that this structure will be 
expanded any time soon.

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-03-10T18:32:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dusgnf02lou@news2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <47bavkFemhcfU1@individual.net>`

```

In article <47bavkFemhcfU1@individual.net>, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> writes:
> Anyone for "associate arrays"?

*Associative* arrays, I think.  "Associate arrays" would be arrays
that are looking forward to another promotion.  :-)

> In the language D you can do the following:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> func(b["hello"]);	// pass 3 as parameter to func()

Yes, D has a bunch of nice features.

Associative arrays are typically found in scripting languages like
Awk and Perl, but they're also found in some general-purpose
languages.  They're usually really just hash tables with array-like
syntax, though other implementations are possible.

To tie this back to another subtopic, associative arrays can be used
to implement decision tables - so they can be a replacement for COBOL
EVALUATE.  The most general approach is to create an associative
array of functions or (for non-functional languages) function
references.
```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:Am5Of.97656$B94.27605@pd7tw3no...

> Surprised REWRITE was problematical for somebody as savvy as you.
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> What's causing you confusion Oliver ?

    I thought you issued a REWRITE on the file, rather than on the record 
associated with the file.

    From my reference document, you can specify a FROM clause for the 
REWRITE statement:

REWRITE record-name-1 FROM identifier-1

REWRITE record-name-1 FROM "literal-1"

    ... with the restriction that record-name-1 and identifier-1 must not 
refer to the same storage area. With my mistaken assumption mentioned above, 
I couldn't figure out what that meant. But now that I see that the 
record-name-1 must indeed be a record, rather than a file, it makes sense.

> --------------------------------------------------
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> performance.

    Thanks. I saw that thread, but had skimmed over it. I'll go back and 
take another look at it.

    - Oliver

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TR4Of.96653$sa3.37735@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<7c6h02ptbgehoemgurlne9h0sb2adsdr11@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <pl0Of.95615$sa3.25753@pd7tw1no> <7c6h02ptbgehoemgurlne9h0sb2adsdr11@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Fri, 03 Mar 2006 18:58:29 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
> <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> command in the language.

What 'Command' Howard  ? I was thinking of VERB usage. Granted, if you 
are thinking in terms of INSPECT... REPLACE - then that one is a doozie. 
But it does become explainable given a decent example used with the 
various options. Frankly I consider the LRM the last place of resort on 
an issue like this.

Don't want to tediously plug it, as I have mentioned it before, but back 
in Compuserve days an M/F user was asking a question about the Screen 
-Section. I took an existing M/F example, added a few lines and Bingo ! 
His reply, "An example beats a million words".

a) LRM/Specification - the techneese on what is supposed to happen
b) Example - this is how you use it

Jimmy

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:stio025bi4n4d6oact0gpte6p1vo560spi@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 04 Mar 2006 00:06:11 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
> <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> English users or to those using other computer languages.

    Admittedly, when you put the literal in the EVALUATE and the variables 
in the WHEN clause, it can be confusing for an English speaker. E.g.:

EVALUATE TRUE
  WHEN a < 3

versus

EVALUATE a < 3
  WHEN TRUE

    but I think if you've done programming in some other languages, you 
might be initially surprised by this, but could quickly cope with it. 
Similar idioms exist in other languages:

In C:

if (3 == x)

versus

if (x == 3)

In Java:

if (myString.equals("foo"))

versus

if ("foo".equals(myString))

etc.

    - Oliver
```

>
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

Hmm, I don't find the above confusing at all.  In fact, in my "dream"
language I would not have an IF statement.  I would have a "test" statement,
something like:

test a eq b
when true
    doasisb
    domore
when false
    doaisnotb
    doevenmore
end test

test a eq b
unless true
    doasisnotb
    doevenmore
end test

test a
when 0
    continue
when 1
when 2
when 4 thru 10
    a += 1
    doanything
when 20 thru 50
    a -= 1
    donothing
otherwise
    a = b
    doafinalalternative
end test

test true
when a < 5
    dosomething
    doasecondthing
when a < 10
    dosomethingelse
when a < 20
    doyetanotherthing
    doasecondthing
otherwise
    dodefault
end test


for simple cases where there's only a single statement to be executed for
the IF/ELSE I'd have...

when a eq b and a not eq c
    doasisbandnotc
otherwise
    dodefault

unless a eq c
    doasisnotb

Feel free to ask me why.  :-)

>Similar idioms exist in other languages:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>etc.

Hmm, unless these are "trick" questions, I don't find either of these to be
confusing...

Anyway...
Frank

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-07T14:59:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ddhPf.16193$vC4.15524@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <pl0Of.95615$sa3.25753@pd7tw1no> <7c6h02ptbgehoemgurlne9h0sb2adsdr11@4ax.com> <TR4Of.96653$sa3.37735@pd7tw1no> <stio025bi4n4d6oact0gpte6p1vo560spi@4ax.com> <uK%Of.15267$M52.6839@edtnps89> <47439hFd96qvU1@individual.net>`

```

"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
news:47439hFd96qvU1@individual.net...
> >
>>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hmm, I don't find the above confusing at all.

    You don't find them confusing *now*, or you never found them confusing, 
even when you first encountered it?

    Either way, if you actually read the statement out loud, I was claiming 
that the second one sounded form sounds more like English than the first 
one.

    "Evalute a less than three. When true, do this. When false, do that."

    "Evaluate false." (already, I suspect that here the non-programmer 
listener will be thinking "huh?") "When a less than three, do this." (and 
here, the if the listener misunderstood the first part, they might try to 
ignore it, and try to recover on the "when" clause, and thus do the reverse 
of what you want) "When a greater or equal to three, do that."

[snip]
>>Similar idioms exist in other languages:
>>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> confusing...

    Again, it's a question of natural-sounding English. This is subjective 
of course, but I claim that most people will find "if x is equal to three" 
more natural than "if three is equal to x".

    The "if (3 == x)" is an idiom in C because in C, assignment statements 
are legal. So a programmer might mistakenly type "if (x = 3)", which will 
MODIFY x (assign it the value 3) as part of the conditional test.

    If the programmer gets in the habit of putting the literal first, the 
equivalent typo would be "if (3 = x)", and the compiler would complain that 
you can't assign to a literal, thus catching the error.

    The Java example has the same issue as above (ordering of the terms 
being compared), but an additional one in that a lot of (beginning?) Java 
programmers don't realize that String literals are themselves objects, and 
so you can use the dereferencing operator '.' on them, and invoke their 
methods.

    The reason for having the literal first is to avoid a 
NullPointerException in the case where myString is null.

    - Oliver
```

James,

> I would however disagree with you about handling strings/text. Even solely 
> within Procedural code there are a tremendous number of features

In 1981(?) I was writing parser (and a kind of preprocessor) for Codasyl 
language.
I've started that in Assembler/370 and pretty soon understood that I'm 
loosing control on my programs,
so I went (back) to PL/I. For this task PL/I definitely better than Cobol 
but you can only imagine the resulting code...
(I didn't know C at that moment and didn't know the existance of the 
LEX/YACC - I was a student)

> one feature in COBOL the EVALUATE is considerably more flexible than the 
> traditional CASE statement in other languages.

I really agree with that statement. Unfortunately this possibility has not 
been implemented in C/C++/Java/C# etc
and I can't find for myself the real reasons why. From my point of view it's 
not a big deal (for example for Java) to allow
construction like switch(Object1)... case Object2: ...
and it's easy for implementation to compare: Obect1.equals(Object2)
I don't know why SUN guys didn't want to do that.

Sorry for not answering your question about java versions - I just didn't 
remember exactly and was too lasy to google but it was short period of time.

Regards,
Sergey

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-06T15:12:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZjYOf.15188$M52.13991@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <pl0Of.95615$sa3.25753@pd7tw1no> <hf6Of.48$UQ1.156825@news.sisna.com>`

```
"Sergey Kashyrin" <ska@resqnet.com> wrote in message 
news:hf6Of.48$UQ1.156825@news.sisna.com...
> From my point of view it's not a big deal (for example for Java) to allow
> construction like switch(Object1)... case Object2: ...
> and it's easy for implementation to compare: Obect1.equals(Object2)
> I don't know why SUN guys didn't want to do that.

    You can already sort-of do that with Java 1.5, as long as these Objects 
are enums.

<example>
public enum Foo {
  a, b, c;

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Foo foo = Foo.a;

    switch(foo) {
    case a:
    case b:
    case c:
      System.out.print("Hello world");
    }
  }
}
</example>

    The problem otherwise is whether whether the implementation should use 
"Object1.equals(Object2)" or "Object1 == Object2". The former is probably 
more useful, but the latter is safer (the first one could throw exceptions 
for example).

    - Oliver
```

```
Oliver,

>    The problem otherwise is whether whether the implementation should use 
> "Object1.equals(Object2)" or "Object1 == Object2". The former is probably 
> more useful, but the latter is safer (the first one could throw exceptions 
> for example).

"Object1 == Object2" will just not work in java as you think !!! :-)))
 in java you can't overload operations and "==" is a pointer comparison and 
not Object.equals function !
i.e.
String a = "abc"; String b = new String(a);
if(a == b) {
        //    I'm giving 100% that you'll never reach here....
}

It's just another 2 cents about java stupidity :-)

Regards,
Sergey

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-06T22:39:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XS2Pf.12046$Cp4.2538@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <pl0Of.95615$sa3.25753@pd7tw1no> <hf6Of.48$UQ1.156825@news.sisna.com> <ZjYOf.15188$M52.13991@edtnps89> <TD2Pf.44$3j.28701@news.sisna.com>`

```

"Sergey Kashyrin" <ska@resqnet.com> wrote in message 
news:TD2Pf.44$3j.28701@news.sisna.com...
> Oliver,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Object1 == Object2" will just not work in java as you think !!! :-)))

    Actually, I suspect it's exactly as I think... but of course, I could be 
wrong.

> in java you can't overload operations and "==" is a pointer comparison and 
> not Object.equals function !
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> }

    I suspect you are correct, but it's tricker than you are implying. The 
compiler will optimize something like this:

String a = "123";
String b = "123";
if (a == b) {
  /*You SHOULD reach this point*/
}

    because it can detect the same literal being used twice, and only 
instantiate a single String object for both the "a" and "b" references. In 
fact, I believe (but am not sure), that the JLS (Java Language 
Specification) REQUIRES that this optimization be done (i.e. if you write a 
compiler which does NOT perform this optimization, then you are not legally 
allowed to call it a "Java compiler").

> It's just another 2 cents about java stupidity :-)

    The == operator and the . operator have two different semantics when 
applied to references. The former is to test for reference equality (i.e. do 
two references refer to the same object?) while the latter is to derefenrece 
members of the object. In the example of (object1.equals(object2)), the . is 
being used to perform a method invocation.

    The method being invoked is called "equals", but there's nothing to 
prevent the programmer from implementing the equals method from, for 
example, printing out the first 20 fibonacci numbers to the standard 
console. (This is a problem with programmers writing incorrect code, rather 
than a problem with Java itself).

    Anyway, the fact that == and .equals does two different things is why I 
bring up the fact that *someone* will have to decide which of these two 
things will be done "under the hood", and that both choices have their 
advantages and disadvantages.

    - Oliver
```

- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <pl0Of.95615$sa3.25753@pd7tw1no> <hf6Of.48$UQ1.156825@news.sisna.com> <ZjYOf.15188$M52.13991@edtnps89> <TD2Pf.44$3j.28701@news.sisna.com> <XS2Pf.12046$Cp4.2538@edtnps90> <i0lPf.78$zF4.184390@news.sisna.com>`

```
[post re-ordered]

"Sergey Kashyrin" <ska@resqnet.com> wrote in message 
news:i0lPf.78$zF4.184390@news.sisna.com...
>
>> String a = "123";
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1.1 I saw different results on a different platforms.

    Might one of those platforms been the MS JVM versus Sun's JVM? Microsoft 
was illegally using the "Java" trademark. Sun permits people to use this 
trademark AS LONG AS your product conforms to their specifications and 
standards document where applicable. MS JVM's didn't follow the Sun's 
standards for JVMs, and so they weren't legally allow to call it a "JVM", 
but they did anyway.

    I looked it up, and found the entry I was thinking of. Section 3.10.5 of 
the 3rd edition of the JLS:
http://java.sun.com/docs/books/jls/third_edition/html/lexical.html#3.10.5

<quote>
Thus, the test program consisting of the compilation unit (ï¿½7.3):

    package testPackage;
    class Test {
            public static void main(String[] args) {
                    String hello = "Hello", lo = "lo";
                    System.out.print((hello == "Hello") + " ");
                    System.out.print((Other.hello == hello) + " ");
                    System.out.print((other.Other.hello == hello) + " ");
                    System.out.print((hello == ("Hel"+"lo")) + " ");
                    System.out.print((hello == ("Hel"+lo)) + " ");
                    System.out.println(hello == ("Hel"+lo).intern());
            }
    }
    class Other { static String hello = "Hello"; }

and the compilation unit:

    package other;
    public class Other { static String hello = "Hello"; }

produces the output:

    true true true true false true

This example illustrates six points:

    * Literal strings within the same class (ï¿½8) in the same package (ï¿½7) 
represent references to the same String object (ï¿½4.3.1).
    * Literal strings within different classes in the same package represent 
references to the same String object.
    * Literal strings within different classes in different packages 
likewise represent references to the same String object.
    * Strings computed by constant expressions (ï¿½15.28) are computed at 
compile time and then treated as if they were literals.
    * Strings computed by concatenation at run time are newly created and 
therefore distinct.
</quote>

    So this optimization is indeed a requirement before you can call your 
platform a "Java platform".

    - Oliver
```

> And if we imagine implementation of switch(Object1) ... case Object2: in

may well be useful to use .equals for String objects, or for String
attributes of the objects, but for, say, a stock object do you mean the
same product code, or the same batch, or the same quantity or ..

```
Richard<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> 03/07/06 2:11 PM >>>
> am not even sure that anyone wants evaluate to be included in Java or
>any other language, it has its own idiom.

Without something similar to COBOL's EVALUATE you must, in Java, do
something like....

if (myString == "one") {
    one();
}
else if (myString == "two") {
    two();
}
else if (myString == "three") {
    three();
}
else {
    four();
}

Wouldn't it be nicer to say:
switch (myString) {
case "one":
    one();
    break;
case "two":
    two();
    break;
case "three":
    three();
    break;
default:
    four();
    break;
}

(Actually, eliminating those silly 'breaks' woul be even nicer, but...)

Of course the above assumes we're comparing the value of the object myString
and not the object itself.  I'm now totally confused as to which does
what...

Frank


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **In-Reply-To:** `<47972lFeicknU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <pl0Of.95615$sa3.25753@pd7tw1no> <hf6Of.48$UQ1.156825@news.sisna.com> <ZjYOf.15188$M52.13991@edtnps89> <TD2Pf.44$3j.28701@news.sisna.com> <XS2Pf.12046$Cp4.2538@edtnps90> <i0lPf.78$zF4.184390@news.sisna.com> <1141765893.159905.119710@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <47972lFeicknU1@individual.net>`

```

Frank Swarbrick wrote:

> Without something similar to COBOL's EVALUATE you must, in Java, do
> something like....
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> }

Actually you probably must not do that as it is not likely to do what
you intend.

> Wouldn't it be nicer to say:
> switch (myString) {
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> }

No. Not nicer.  I use Cobol to write Cobol programs.  There is no case
statement at all in Python and I don't miss it.

> (Actually, eliminating those silly 'breaks' woul be even nicer, but...)

The 'breaks' are not 'silly'.  They allow one to do:

    case 1:
    case 2:
             dopart1and2;
             break;

or even drop through:

    case 1:
             dopart1only;
    case 2:
             dopart1and2;
             break;

But the break, or lack of it, is one of the bug traps in C-like
languges. In Cobol one of the bug traps is not having an imperitive
statement after a WHEN, or commenting it out, and having this drop into
the next action.  Python eliminated the case statement exactly for the
reason that doing so removes the opportunity to create bugs.

> Of course the above assumes we're comparing the value of the object myString
> and not the object itself.  I'm now totally confused as to which does
> what...

An == operator will only compare primitives. Object references are
primitives, so Object == Object is only true if the two object
references are identical, ie they both reference the same object.
```

Richard<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> 03/08/06 6:09 PM >>>
>
>Frank Swarbrick wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>you intend.

You're right.  As I said, I couldn't recall if == would compare string
values or just their references.  In C# it compares values.

>> Wouldn't it be nicer to say:
>> switch (myString) {
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>statement at all in Python and I don't miss it.

I don't know Python, really, other than a little reading.  Can you show me
how you might code the equivalent of a case statement?

>> (Actually, eliminating those silly 'breaks' woul be even nicer, but...)
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>reason that doing so removes the opportunity to create bugs.

I think that one is much more likely to forget a 'break' statement than one
is to comment out a single COBOL statement and having it drop through. 
Which is not to say that there isn't a third, better, way.  Such as,
maybe...

switch {
case 1:
    or 2:
    or 3:
      dopart1;
case 4:
      dopart2;
      fallthru;
default:
      doallothers;
}

Just off the top of my head, but this might be just as flexible and less
prone to mistakes.

>> Of course the above assumes we're comparing the value of the object
myString
>> and not the object itself.  I'm now totally confused as to which does
>> what...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>references are identical, ie they both reference the same object.

Yep.  As I said in a previous message, I don't like this.  It seems to me
I'm much more likely to want to do a "value" compare than a "reference"
compare, so to me the "value" compare should be the "basic" compare.  Just
my opinion, obviously.

Frank


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-09T13:49:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1141940961.111078.12880@j52g2000cwj.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<47bjmfFeshr1U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <pl0Of.95615$sa3.25753@pd7tw1no> <hf6Of.48$UQ1.156825@news.sisna.com> <ZjYOf.15188$M52.13991@edtnps89> <TD2Pf.44$3j.28701@news.sisna.com> <XS2Pf.12046$Cp4.2538@edtnps90> <i0lPf.78$zF4.184390@news.sisna.com> <1141765893.159905.119710@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <47972lFeicknU1@individual.net> <1141866554.336009.123450@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <47bjmfFeshr1U1@individual.net>`

```

Frank Swarbrick wrote:

> You're right.  As I said, I couldn't recall if == would compare string
> values or just their references.  In C# it compares values.

It may, or may not, compare values depending on what is defined for
that class and what has been overloaded.

For example it may be that if you had:

       if ( stringobject == 23 )

This may compare the length of the string with the integer, such is the
wonder of overloaded operators. The point being that you cannot tell
what it is going to be doing without looking at the class or
documentation for it.

Which is why I dislike overloading of operators.

> I don't know Python, really, other than a little reading.  Can you show me
> how you might code the equivalent of a case statement?

Just a series of if .. elif .. elif .. else. In python the == operator
does compare strings so:

    if ( control == ¨one¨ ):
        one()
    elif ( control == ¨two¨ ):
        two()
    else:
        thisdefault()

> I think that one is much more likely to forget a 'break' statement than one
> is to comment out a single COBOL statement and having it drop through.

That may depend on how experienced one is in the various languages and
how easily one switches from one to the other. I can´t recall the last
time that I forgot a break, but I do recall having done it when I
started with C.

> Which is not to say that there isn't a third, better, way.  Such as,
> maybe...

But why not just be explicit about what you actually want with:

    if ( this == ¨one¨
     or this == ¨two¨ ):
          do_one_or_two()
     elif ( ...

> Just off the top of my head, but this might be just as flexible and less
> prone to mistakes.

If it is _different_ then it will lead to mistakes.  Java is fine for C
programmers _because_ it is the same.

> Yep.  As I said in a previous message, I don't like this.  It seems to me
> I'm much more likely to want to do a "value" compare than a "reference"
> compare, so to me the "value" compare should be the "basic" compare.  Just
> my opinion, obviously.

Then don´t use Java or C.  In C one would use if ( strcmp(string1,
string2) == 0 ) or similar to compare two strings. If ( string1 ==
string2 ) compares the two addresses. It is the way that the language
works, if you don´t like it then choose another. Personally, I have no
trouble switching to use what is provided by the language I am using at
the time.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PhXPf.15505$Cp4.96@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <pl0Of.95615$sa3.25753@pd7tw1no> <hf6Of.48$UQ1.156825@news.sisna.com> <ZjYOf.15188$M52.13991@edtnps89> <TD2Pf.44$3j.28701@news.sisna.com> <XS2Pf.12046$Cp4.2538@edtnps90> <i0lPf.78$zF4.184390@news.sisna.com> <1141765893.159905.119710@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <47972lFeicknU1@individual.net>`

```
[post slightly re-ordered]

"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
news:47972lFeicknU1@individual.net...
> Richard<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> 03/07/06 2:11 PM >>>
>> am not even sure that anyone wants evaluate to be included in Java or
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> what...

    As Richard said, == compares references ("pointer addresses" if you want 
to think of that way, though in a technical/pedantic manner, that's not 
strictly correct), while .equals() invokes the "equals" method, which can be 
programmed to do anything you want. In the case of Strings, "equals" 
compares the contents of the strings for equality (in a case-sensitive 
manner).

    So in Java, you'd probably be writing:

if ("one".equals(myString)) {
  one();
} else if ("two".equals(myString)) {
  two();
}
/*etc.*/


>
> Wouldn't it be nicer to say:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> }

    In the special case of Java, I'd say "yes". The reason is that Java 
already made the mistake (well, it's a mistake in my opinion) of sometimes 
treating Strings like primitives, and other times treating them like 
Objects. The above kind of construct perpetuates this illusion that Strings 
are like primitives.

    So "normally", I'd say "no", and that the above construct would detract 
from the (mathematical) elegance of the language. However, since Java has 
already went down the road of treating Strings like primitive, the above 
construct won't add much more harm, and does provide some convenience to the 
programmer, so my final answer is "Yes, that'd be nice."

>
> (Actually, eliminating those silly 'breaks' woul be even nicer, but...)

    I agree. I'd like for the default behaviour to be a break, and if a 
fall-through is desired, for that to be explicitly said. E.g.

switch (myInteger) {
  case 1:
    handleOne();
  case 2:
    fallthrough;
  case 3:
    fallthrough;
  case 4:
    handleTwoToFourButNotFive();
    fallthrough;
  case 5:
    handleTwoToFive();
  default:
    handleDefaultCase();
}

    - Oliver
```

>

>    As Richard said, == compares references ("pointer addresses" if you
want 
>to think of that way, though in a technical/pedantic manner, that's not 
>strictly correct), while .equals() invokes the "equals" method, which can
be 
>programmed to do anything you want. In the case of Strings, "equals" 
>compares the contents of the strings for equality (in a case-sensitive 
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>already made the mistake (well, it's a mistake in my opinion) of sometimes

>treating Strings like primitives, and other times treating them like 
>Objects. The above kind of construct perpetuates this illusion that Strings

>are like primitives.
>
>    So "normally", I'd say "no", and that the above construct would detract

>from the (mathematical) elegance of the language. However, since Java has 
>already went down the road of treating Strings like primitive, the above 
>construct won't add much more harm, and does provide some convenience to
the 
>programmer, so my final answer is "Yes, that'd be nice."

I understand the reasoning behind '==' comparing the references.  I just
don't agree with it.  Meaning, I think to most people just starting
programming, with little understanding of a "reference object" versus a
primative type, would assume that "if (string1 == string2)" would be
comparing the "values" of the strings, and whether or not the two strings
refer to the same object.  Personally, I'd treat == of two objects as an
alias for the "equals" method and have something else be used for a
reference comparison.  Perhaps "if (@string1 == @string2)" or something... 


As a matter of fact, this is pretty much how C# works
if (s1 == s2): compares the values of the two strings

if ((object) s1 == (object) s2): compares the "references" of the two
strings

Not sure if this is true only for strings or for all objects...

>> (Actually, eliminating those silly 'breaks' woul be even nicer, but...)
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>}

Indeed.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-09T17:49:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GUZPf.15532$Cp4.11924@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <pl0Of.95615$sa3.25753@pd7tw1no> <hf6Of.48$UQ1.156825@news.sisna.com> <ZjYOf.15188$M52.13991@edtnps89> <TD2Pf.44$3j.28701@news.sisna.com> <XS2Pf.12046$Cp4.2538@edtnps90> <i0lPf.78$zF4.184390@news.sisna.com> <1141765893.159905.119710@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <47972lFeicknU1@individual.net> <PhXPf.15505$Cp4.96@edtnps90> <47b5eeFef0qkU1@individual.net>`

```
"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
news:47b5eeFef0qkU1@individual.net...
>
> I understand the reasoning behind '==' comparing the references.  I just
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not sure if this is true only for strings or for all objects...

    C# implements comparing the contents of strings via operator 
overloading. That is, in C#, you as, a programmer, can change the behaviour 
of the "==" operator. I suspect that the default behaviour of the == 
operator in C# is to compare references, but the person who wrote the String 
class overrode the operator to instead compare the contents of the string.

    One problem with this is that it's not always clear, for two given 
objects, whether they should be considered equal; rather, it depends a lot 
on context. With strings, for example, it's common to want to perform a 
case-insensitive comparison. You might claim that it's trivial to implement 
your own version of case-insensitive comparison by simply converting both 
strings to uppercase first, then doing the comparison.

    The problem with this, however, is that it's not always clear what the 
uppercase of a given character is. The German character with Unicode 
Codepoint \u00DF is considered a "lowercase" character. When converted to 
uppercase, it sometimes becomes "SS", and other times becomes "SZ".

    That means, for example, if you want to do a case-insentive comparison 
between "\u00DF\u00DF" and "SSSZ", you can't simply convert the first string 
to uppercase and then doing the comparison, as the transformation to 
uppercase migth result in "SSSS", and then the comparison would return 
false, as "SSSS" != "SSSZ", whereas "SSSZ" is indeed equal to "\u00DF\u00DF" 
in a case-insensitive comparison.

    This problem isn't limited only to Strings... For any kind of objects, 
there might be multiple definitions of "equality", depending on the context. 
Are two files considered "equal" if their contents are the same, but their 
last-modified timestamps differ? Is the value 5.0 considered "equal" to 5.00 
(was the extra zero an indication of more significant digits due to more 
accurate measurement, or just a whim of the user entering the data)? Etc.

    Java sort of has a solution to this problem. They have an class called a 
Comparator, which is I guess sort of like COLLATING SEQUENCEs in COBOL, 
where you can define the exact behaviour for whether objects are less than, 
equal, or greater than each other. You could have the exact same set of 
objects (e.g. a bunch of files), and have two Comparators acting on them, so 
that sometimes the timestamp matters, and sometimes it doesn't, depending on 
the context.

    The only problem with Comparators is that it enforces a total ordering 
on the objects. That is, for every pair of objects of the same type, the 
comparator must either say "Less than", "Equal to" or "Greater Than". 
Because the objects must be of the same type, you don't have to worry about 
comparing numbers to files for example. But it does mean that you cannot 
simply say an Apple is "Not Equal" to an Orange; instead, you'd have to 
actually specify one of them as being "less than" the other.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

strings
>> refer to the same object.  Personally, I'd treat == of two objects as an
>> alias for the "equals" method and have something else be used for a
>> reference comparison.  Perhaps "if (@string1 == @string2)" or
something...
>>
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>overloading. That is, in C#, you as, a programmer, can change the behaviour

>of the "==" operator. I suspect that the default behaviour of the == 
>operator in C# is to compare references, but the person who wrote the
String 
>class overrode the operator to instead compare the contents of the string.
>
>    One problem with this is that it's not always clear, for two given 
>objects, whether they should be considered equal; rather, it depends a lot

>on context. With strings, for example, it's common to want to perform a 
>case-insensitive comparison. You might claim that it's trivial to implement

>your own version of case-insensitive comparison by simply converting both 
>strings to uppercase first, then doing the comparison.
>
>    The problem with this, however, is that it's not always clear what the

>uppercase of a given character is. The German character with Unicode 
>Codepoint \u00DF is considered a "lowercase" character. When converted to 
>uppercase, it sometimes becomes "SS", and other times becomes "SZ".
>
>    That means, for example, if you want to do a case-insentive comparison

>between "\u00DF\u00DF" and "SSSZ", you can't simply convert the first
string 
>to uppercase and then doing the comparison, as the transformation to 
>uppercase migth result in "SSSS", and then the comparison would return 
>false, as "SSSS" != "SSSZ", whereas "SSSZ" is indeed equal to
"\u00DF\u00DF" 
>in a case-insensitive comparison.
>
>    This problem isn't limited only to Strings... For any kind of objects,

>there might be multiple definitions of "equality", depending on the
context. 
>Are two files considered "equal" if their contents are the same, but their

>last-modified timestamps differ? Is the value 5.0 considered "equal" to
5.00 
>(was the extra zero an indication of more significant digits due to more 
>accurate measurement, or just a whim of the user entering the data)? Etc.
>
>    Java sort of has a solution to this problem. They have an class called
a 
>Comparator, which is I guess sort of like COLLATING SEQUENCEs in COBOL, 
>where you can define the exact behaviour for whether objects are less than,

>equal, or greater than each other. You could have the exact same set of 
>objects (e.g. a bunch of files), and have two Comparators acting on them,
so 
>that sometimes the timestamp matters, and sometimes it doesn't, depending
on 
>the context.
>
>    The only problem with Comparators is that it enforces a total ordering

>on the objects. That is, for every pair of objects of the same type, the 
>comparator must either say "Less than", "Equal to" or "Greater Than". 
>Because the objects must be of the same type, you don't have to worry about

>comparing numbers to files for example. But it does mean that you cannot 
>simply say an Apple is "Not Equal" to an Orange; instead, you'd have to 
>actually specify one of them as being "less than" the other.

Given all that, I would say that for any class where '==' is to be used the
class must implement an '==s' method.  Which is somewhat how C# is doing it,
except that apparently the default of '==' is to do object comparisons, and
only if you overload it will it do something else.  In my 'perfect language'
(hah) if you attempted do say "if a == b" where a and be were objects, if
the '==' method was not implemented then the statement would either not
compile or would throw a runtime exception.  There are, of course, probably
many cases where comparing the values of two objects does not even make
sense.  So using '==' in these cases would be an error.

Frank

--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

Sergey Kashyrin wrote:
> String a = "abc"; String b = new String(a);
> if(a == b) {
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's just another 2 cents about java stupidity :-)

It's not a 'Java stupidity', it does exactly what it says it does, and
exactly what a C programmer would expect it to do over the last few
decades.

   String a = "abc";
   String b = a;

   if ( a == b ) { this is reached }

It seems that what you call a 'Java stupidity' may just be an
unwillingness to learn.

> It's not a 'Java stupidity', it does exactly what it says it does, and

Of course it does exactly what it should do and "stupidity" is not about 
that but about inability of java language to provide flexibility and power 
to me at the level C++ and C# provide.
Of course as it was already said java changes and getting better, but for 
example it took almost 10 years for its creators to allow collection objects 
to hold primitive datatypes :-)))
Maybe in a next 10 years we will have an equivalent of Cobol EVALUATE there 
:-)

Regards,
Sergey

"Sergey Kashyrin" <ska@resqnet.com> wrote in message 
news:P1lPf.79$66.157848@news.sisna.com...
> Richard,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to me at the level C++ and C# provide.

    I don't think it's an "inability", but rather a "conscious design 
decision". Personally, I'd be disapointed if it a future version of Java 
allows operator overloading.

> Of course as it was already said java changes and getting better, but for 
> example it took almost 10 years for its creators to allow collection 
> objects to hold primitive datatypes :-)))

    Technically, you STILL can't put primitives into collections. The 
compiler will, behind the scenes, wrap the the primitive into a boxing 
object of the appropriate type (e.g. placing ints into Integers). Before 1.5 
(where this behind the scenes "feature" was introduced), you could have 
manually done the boxing yourself, so really there is no expressive power 
gained by this; it's just syntactic sugar.

> Maybe in a next 10 years we will have an equivalent of Cobol EVALUATE 
> there :-)

    - Oliver
```

- **Message-ID:** `<1141766527.775258.137830@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<P1lPf.79$66.157848@news.sisna.com>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <pl0Of.95615$sa3.25753@pd7tw1no> <hf6Of.48$UQ1.156825@news.sisna.com> <ZjYOf.15188$M52.13991@edtnps89> <TD2Pf.44$3j.28701@news.sisna.com> <1141685423.139673.93940@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <P1lPf.79$66.157848@news.sisna.com>`

```

Sergey Kashyrin wrote:
> that but about inability of java language to provide flexibility and power
> to me at the level C++ and C# provide.

That is a design feature not an 'inabilty'. C++ and C# provide such
things as arbitrary pointers.  Power but insecurity.  If you want that
then use those languages.

> Of course as it was already said java changes and getting better, but for
> example it took almost 10 years for its creators to allow collection objects
> to hold primitive datatypes :-)))

It always could, they just needed to be wrapped. A colloction class is
a collection of _objects_ and thus requires an object reference to
determine what each object is and how to deal with it. A raw primitive
does not have an object reference.

> Maybe in a next 10 years we will have an equivalent of Cobol EVALUATE there
> :-)

No. You won't.  You may get a decision table preprocessor if someone
could be bothered to write one.

If you want to use evaluate then write in Cobol.
```

```
Sergey Kashyrin<ska@resqnet.com> 03/02/06 6:28 PM >>>
>James,
>
>I agree with your point that Java is (was) too young and timeframe of those

>changes are relatively short.
>So you had to adjust your client app constantly, even for minor versions 
>sometime.
>I'm not against Java at all and as of now I'm using it about 50% of my
time.
>
>I just don't know what will happen next. So far for server applications all

>those incompatibilities were not serious issues.
>More problems are on a client side where you (as a developer) can't control

>the version of Java, but of course any serious developers team can overcome

>that.
>I expect in a next few years Java will be more stable and it will be less 
>number of incompatibilities issues, so the idea to reuse somebody's 
>component (or free component libraries) in Java is where the world is
going.
>
>Regarding .Net - I really don't have a "production" client app yet (I have

>it as a test in development in both J# and converting to C# using MS 
>conversion assistant). And the main reason is just that nobody asked for it

>yet. I'm not for MS and I'm not against it. I just have to work with it 
>because 99.99% of the "client side" are sitting on win2k/xp/etc. and from a

>business point of view you want to get advantage of that fact.
>I'm in the position where I can't dictate the client what to use, so I have

>to support (i.e. app has to run) ANY possible platform.
>Personally I prefer C# over Java but a little bit (I like operation
overload 
>in Algol-68, ADA and C++).
>
>Speaking of Cobol, I'm not a Cobol programmer (of coures I know it but just

>to support a huge existing app) - I've started from Algol-60, PL/I,
Fortran, 
>old HP Basic and a lot of different assemblers, then mostly went to C/C++ 
>(before java in 96).
>The problem of Cobol is that you can't do some tasks efficiently (i.e. text

>processing etc) and I'm not sure that implementation of object model in 
>Cobol gives you relatively readable code (probably it is for a Cobol 
>programmer, but for me it's easier to think in terms of java or C++
classes)

I've been a COBOL programmer for almost 10 years (yes, still but a child...)
and from what I've seen of OO COBOL it *is* hard to read; not to mention
excessively verbose.  I mean really, what's easier to read:

invoke myObject "MyMethod" 
    using myParm 
    returning myResult

or

myResult = myObject.MyMethod(myParm)

?
Actually, neither is that hard to read, but the latter is much easier on my
fingers.  Still...  It took me until just a few weeks ago to understand that
a "factory" method is just a "static" method in other languages.

I also don't like having all the different "divisions" and stuff.  It's just
a lot of extra words that, to me, actually make it less readable.

Maybe it's just me...  I learned C++ before I ever saw OO-COBOL, and I've
never actually *used* OO-COBOL, but it's my impression that it's a bit too
little a bit too late...  Perhaps I should get MF Education Edition (or
whatever its called) just to try it out.  Maybe I'll change my mind.

Frank

--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2006-03-04T05:44:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rP9Of.98593$sa3.14810@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<46sb84Fcgn9iU3@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <46sb84Fcgn9iU3@individual.net>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> I've been a COBOL programmer for almost 10 years (yes, still but a child...)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> myResult = myObject.MyMethod(myParm)

I agree the above Java syntax is easy to read but *because* I'm into OO 
COBOL. Not having studied Java, I wont go beyond that statement.

Step back a bit - OO COBOL syntax was written to satisfy the needs of 
Procedural COBOL programmers - not you snotty-nosed kids trained in the 
cryptic languages :-). It would never have flown. some 19/20 words added 
to COBOL to cope with OO, and give it a 'look and feel' like Procedural. 
Even so, it hasn't taken off - how could it mainframe wise with no 
compiler using it. Enterprise is a comparative newcomer and as you have 
pointed out has caveats when using OO.

Without question - COBOL is unduly verbose, but in my view not too 
verbose in using words like "invoke, using, returning". I used to write 
shorthand many moons ago, but much prefer E-N-G-L-I-S-H.

I commented on it before, but it will never go anywhere. Take the 
following which is allowable in M/F and is shorter than examples you 
will see in the Standard or as often used by Fujitsu programmers. The 
comment lines are my personal decoration for clarity :-

*>-------------------------------
Method-id. "GetSomeObject"
*>-------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 MyParam		pic 9(03).
Linkage section.
01 MyObject		object reference.
01 lnk-Result		pic s9(06)v9(02).

Procedure Division using MyObject returning lnk-Object.

move 22 to MyParam
invoke MyObject "MyMethod" using MyParam returning lnk-Result

End Method "GetSomeObject".
*>--------------------------------------

Given above is the requirement, which is what newcomers would want to 
see, say when switching from Procedural, it could finish up like this :-

{GetSomeObject.
	01 MyParam pic 9(03).
	Linkage section.
	01 MyObject object reference.
	01 lnk-Result	pic s9(06)v9(02).
         Procedure using MyObject returning lnk-Object.
	move 22 to MyParam
	invoke MyObject "MyMethod" using MyParam returning lnk-Result
}

Note - Don't need 'Method-id', nor do I need the End Method and the End 
Method literal. Local-storage I don't need - the header is understood. I 
don't need Linkage section unless I use it. And I only need Procedure 
when using or returning. So, if you like you can consider 'Linkage' and 
'Procedure' to be a complimentary pair

Nowhere as terse as your example, but probably about the best shot you 
could get using COBOL shorthand, and I can't think it would be a 
challenge for a compiler to recognize above. My original and the above 
would dovetail into a normal Procedural compiler. However, try and 
abbreviate some more and I think you are talking an entirely separate 
compiler geared to OO - wish they had done that anyway, now I've seen 
where this whole mess has gone ! (On reflection perhaps a Directive in 
the source could direct the compiler to tell it that it is validating OO 
code).

> Actually, neither is that hard to read, but the latter is much easier on my
> fingers.  Still...  It took me until just a few weeks ago to understand that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> a lot of extra words that, to me, actually make it less readable.

Couldn't agree more and M/F let you get around that with their 'ignore 
the red tape'. Just don't understand why some of the others didn't pick 
up on that as a feature.

> 
> Maybe it's just me...  I learned C++ before I ever saw OO-COBOL, and I've
> never actually *used* OO-COBOL, but it's my impression that it's a bit too
> little a bit too late...  Perhaps I should get MF Education Edition (or
> whatever its called) just to try it out.  Maybe I'll change my mind.

It's not 'too little', adequately covered by both F/J and M/F in their 
own respective ways. But I've now concluded it is 'too late'.

If in hobbyist mode wouldn't hurt to get University Edition - watch the 
M/F site a few months hence for when they release V 5.0; the UE should 
be available about the same time. I still think it is a SUPERB PRODUCT,
but if you wanna get serious and use it in the real world to sell apps - 
don't forget those friggin' runtime fees.

PS: Funny how you get the wrong impression. Seeing somebody called Frank
Swarbrick writing, and from a bank, I formed the impression this is an 
'old salt' with about three decades under his belt. As you admit, but a 
mere child :-)

Jimmy
```

```
James J. Gavan<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> 03/03/06 10:44 PM >>>
>Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>> 
>> I've been a COBOL programmer for almost 10 years (yes, still but a
child...)
>> and from what I've seen of OO COBOL it *is* hard to read; not to mention
>> excessively verbose.  I mean really, what's easier to read:
…[73 more quoted lines elided]…
>code).

I'm actually not fond of the curly braces.  I prefer the word 'end' to
designate the end of a block.  (In some ways I like Python, where blocks are
formed with the indentation, so neither block delimiters nor 'end'
statements are necessary.  But not having actually programmed in Python I'm
not sure if it eliminates or causes errors because of this.)  I like words
for "new" situations, but once I get used to it I am fine with symbols.  For
instance, I like "i++" far more than "add 1 to I", and I definitely like "a
= b + c" more than "add a to b giving c".  (Of course we do have "COMPUTE c
= a + b", but that extra verb is pointless...)

I have a Mac, so I've been looking at Objective C for the last few days. 
Interesting, but I have a problem with:

+ mymethod
{
    return self
}

- mymethod
{
    return self
}

The difference between the two is that "+ mymethod" is a static, or factory,
or class method (choose the descriptor you understand most), while "-
mymethod" is an instance method.  My point being there is, though more
verbose, wouldn't using "class" instead of "+", and "instance" instead of
"-" be easier to understand?

So I keep going back and forth.  COBOL is too verbose, but C and the like
are too terse.  Ah well...

>> Actually, neither is that hard to read, but the latter is much easier on
my
>> fingers.  Still...  It took me until just a few weeks ago to understand
that
>> a "factory" method is just a "static" method in other languages.
>> 
>> I also don't like having all the different "divisions" and stuff.  It's
just
>> a lot of extra words that, to me, actually make it less readable.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Maybe it's just me...  I learned C++ before I ever saw OO-COBOL, and
I've
>> never actually *used* OO-COBOL, but it's my impression that it's a bit
too
>> little a bit too late...  Perhaps I should get MF Education Edition (or
>> whatever its called) just to try it out.  Maybe I'll change my mind.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>mere child :-)

What is significant about my name that would lead you to believe I was
older?

As for programming experience, I have been programming since I learned BASIC
in junior high, so that is about 25 years...

Frank

- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <46sb84Fcgn9iU3@individual.net> <rP9Of.98593$sa3.14810@pd7tw1no> <476d3bFe2lamU1@individual.net>`

```

"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
news:476d3bFe2lamU1@individual.net...

> I have a Mac, so I've been looking at Objective C for the last few days.
> Interesting, but I have a problem with:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> are too terse.  Ah well...

    If you like a particular language, but just don't like minor syntactical 
decisions (like what the keywords are), it isn't too difficult (maybe a 
couple of weeks or months work) to design your own language "on top of" your 
favorite language.

    You could write a compiler which took:

<input>
class mymethod
{
    return self
}

instance mymethod
{
    return self
}
</input>

and emitted:

<output>
+ mymethod
{
    return self
}

- mymethod
{
    return self
}
</output>

    Then you'd just right a quick script to sequentially call your compiler, 
and then the Objective C compiler, and you're all set.

    - Oliver
```

Oliver Wong wrote:

>     If you like a particular language, but just don't like minor syntactical
> decisions (like what the keywords are), it isn't too difficult (maybe a
> couple of weeks or months work) to design your own language "on top of" your
> favorite language.

And that is one of the strengths and weaknesses of (some) OO languages.
You can effectively write your own language in OO by using operator
overloading.
```

Oliver Wong wrote:
> 
> "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>> "-" be easier to understand?

Definitely agree with you there - why convolute it, when the addition of 
the odd word would give clarity.

>> So I keep going back and forth.  COBOL is too verbose, but C and the like
>> are too terse.  Ah well...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> language "on top of" your favorite language.

On that tack - Micro Focus does have a class Vocabulary, which I don't 
use. I haven't seen others mention it, although unknowingly when 
developers are copy/pasting they are using Vocabulary 'substitutes'. See 
a brief outline here :-

http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/nx40/nx40indx.htm

This is an M/F provided support class - not your own baby - but it does 
allow you to use the syntax to write your own set of 'substitutes'. 
Follow the format and the vocabulary files are acceptable to the compiler.

Jimmy
```

```

"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
news:478s73Feg61bU3@individual.net...
 >
> Indeed you could
[invent your own language]
>.  But then perhaps no one else could read your "new"
> language because it's defined only in your head (and in the source code of
> the compiler), so I'm not sure that's a great idea...

    Just so you don't think this is some looney I just came up with out of 
nowhere <grin>...

    We do that semi-frequently here where I work; invent new domain-specific 
languages, that is, because (in the long run) it'll end up being faster than 
directly coding it in Java, for example.

    Also, from what I've heard, when programmers design a new game engine, 
they might write the core in C or C++, but they'll frequently invent a new 
"scripting" language that the artist, level designers, etc. can use to have 
to trigger events in the game world. Sometimes, the game companies even 
release their internal level-editing programs to the public so that fans can 
design their own levels and distribute on the Internet (essentially, it's 
free publicity for the game), so these fans will occasionally encounter this 
new custom programming language and have to learn it.

    - Oliver
```

- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <dtnp9a02ht9@news1.newsguy.com> <RJMLf.67898$H%4.24314@pd7tw2no> <469v39Fa4immU1@individual.net> <p1a802dpn9sut0uu4a1opqg619j3jdjkri@4ax.com> <1208nvo5pt6vac8@news.supernews.com> <l6o902l6tm4ujtr03fektdi84cu41i7ip9@4ax.com> <46mc3rFbb7s3U1@individual.net> <aocc02tl92s4b6i6dhcb4k1rj2ubjgi6qd@4ax.com> <jFsNf.32$aG2.121976@news.sisna.com> <ARvNf.83587$sa3.6227@pd7tw1no> <KkENf.11720$dg.5162@clgrps13> <VJFNf.49$kS2.132226@news.sisna.com> <PdLNf.87787$B94.9390@pd7tw3no> <vZMNf.77$zs4.175274@news.sisna.com> <46sb84Fcgn9iU3@individual.net> <rP9Of.98593$sa3.14810@pd7tw1no> <476d3bFe2lamU1@individual.net>`

```

Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>   (In some ways I like Python, where blocks are
> formed with the indentation, so neither block delimiters nor 'end'
> statements are necessary.  But not having actually programmed in Python I'm
> not sure if it eliminates or causes errors because of this.)

You do need to be absolutely certain that your editor is consistent
with its use of tab stops. I prefer my editor to always fill with
spaces and to display where actual tab characters exist (with colour
blocks) as mixing tabs and spaces indents will surely cause problems.

Once that is sorted then it comes to be natural that what you see is
what you get, and is what the compiler gets too.  No more tracking of
end-~ or } to check levels.

I suspect that I make fewer errors with Python than with other
languages and certainly have fewer arguments with myself over style.
```

"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
news:46sb84Fcgn9iU3@individual.net...
> Sergey Kashyrin<ska@resqnet.com> 03/02/06 6:28 PM >>>
>>James,
>>
<snip>
> I've been a COBOL programmer for almost 10 years (yes, still but a child...)
> and from what I've seen of OO COBOL it *is* hard to read; not to mention
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> ?

Frank,
  If you aren't aware, the '02 COBOL Standard allows

Move myoBJECT::"MyMethod" (myParm) to MyResult

which seems pretty much like the 2nd example you gave.

For the "COBOL-thinker"
   INVOKE is to CALL
      as
  inline object invocation (ussing "::" operator)  is to user-defined functions

(both of the latter features are fully supported in the '02 COBOL Standard, but 
not in all that many existing implementations)
```

I think it has to do with the differences between

 Call  Pgm-Field-Name
      versus
  Call  "real-name-pgm"

The method portion of the Invoke statement (which does not have a data-name 
option) has the method in quotes (to parallel CALL - and this was then extended 
to inline invocation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-03-08T19:21:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dunas801l4s@news2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <476delFe2lamU2@individual.net>`

```

In article <476delFe2lamU2@individual.net>, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> writes:
> 
> By the way, can someone explain the reason for the quotes around the method
> name?  Seems superfluous, except perhaps to separate the object name from
> the method name and the parameters.

Same reason why you need them around a literal program name in a call
statement.  The method name can be a data-item as well as a literal:

   move "myMethod" to method-name
   invoke myObject method-name ...

This is actually quite different from the C-derived languages, which
support *neither* of those name-resolution methods directly.  In C,
for example, a called function can only be specified using a variable
of a function-pointer type, or the name of a function - which is
actually the name of a function-type variable, and decays to a
pointer-to-function-type variable in most contexts.  (Function-type
variables in C are immutable, so "variable" is a bit of a misnomer,
but from the language's point of view that's what they are.)

C-derived languages thus require other mechanisms to call a function
using a name determined at run-time, and in fact there's no standard
way to do that in C.

Thus in Java you write:

   myObject.myMethod(...);

because you can't write:

   String myMethodName = "myMethod";
   myObject.myMethodName(...);

But in COBOL, in effect, you can do the latter, so it has to be
distinguished from the equivalent of the former.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

```
Michael Wojcik<mwojcik@newsguy.com> 03/08/06 12:21 PM >>>
>
>In article <476delFe2lamU2@individual.net>, "Frank Swarbrick"
<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> writes:
>> 
>> By the way, can someone explain the reason for the quotes around the
method
>> name?  Seems superfluous, except perhaps to separate the object name
from
>> the method name and the parameters.
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>distinguished from the equivalent of the former.

Makes sense!  Thanks for the clarification.

I believe you can do something similar in Java, though.  I just now tried
this.

instead of:
try {
	worksock.connect(s_addr);
}
catch (Exception e) {
	System.out.println(e);
	return;
}

You can do:
try {
	Class[] methodParms = new Class[1];
	methodParms[0] = SocketAddress.class;
	Class socketClass = worksock.getClass();
	Method myMethod = socketClass.getMethod("connect", methodParms);
	Object[] parmObjs = new Object[1];
	parmObjs[0] = s_addr;
                myMethod.invoke(worksock, parmObjs);
}
catch (Exception e) {
	System.out.println(e);
	return;
}

Not that I'd recommend it!  You may be able to simplfy it somewhat, but I'm
not Java expert, so I'm not sure how...

Frank


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-03-09T19:37:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<duq05d01ps5@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <4793b3FekqcoU1@individual.net>`

```

In article <4793b3FekqcoU1@individual.net>, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> writes:
> Michael Wojcik<mwojcik@newsguy.com> 03/08/06 12:21 PM >>>
> >
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 myMethod.invoke(worksock, parmObjs);

Right, but there's the extra indirection of creating a Class object
and a Method object and so forth.  (This is what there's no standard
for in C.  There *is* a standard way to do it in Java, as you've just
described.)

COBOL, on the other hand, has syntactic sugar to do the "look this
name up and resolve it to the code I want to call" bit, right in the
CALL or INVOKE verb.

Languages with first-class functions, like Scheme and OCaml, have yet
another variation on this: you can have function names and function
variables and functions you compute at runtime, and you can pass
functions as parameters and return new functions.  They're even more
flexible when it comes to invoking functions and methods because the
thing they're invoking may not have existed until right before the
invocation.

In summary:

C: You can only call functions known at compile time, directly or
through pointers, in the standard language.

Java: You can call methods using a name known at runtime, but you
have to go through extra hoops to do so.

COBOL: You can call programs and methods directly or by name, but
they have to have been previously compiled.

Functional languages: You can call functions you just made up a
moment ago in the running program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-03-10T18:43:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dushcq01s5v@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<1140796116.074700.17110@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <47docfFf100vU1@individual.net>`

```

In article <47docfFf100vU1@individual.net>, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> writes:
> >>>> Michael Wojcik<mwojcik@newsguy.com> 03/09/06 12:37 PM >>>
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> there are any specific advantages to the Java way.

Well, the C/Java way does give you more control.  You know whether
you're calling a method (or function, depending on the language) that
was specified at compilation time, or something that the runtime
needs to go out and find.  (This may not be strictly true in all C
implementations, because so much is unspecified by the standard.)
With COBOL, CALL "name" hides that distinction, which is convenient
if you don't care, but troublesome if you do.

For example, if I mean to call "foo" in my COBOL program, but type "fo"
by mistake, I won't know about that error unless and until my program
happens to go down that code path at runtime.

> >Languages with first-class functions, like Scheme and OCaml, have yet
> >another variation on this...
> 
> You lost me!  :-)  (Don't bother trying to elaborate.  I'll have to read up
> on functional programming before trying to make sense of it.)

I suspect you'd like it.  You might not want to use it professionally,
but people who are interested in programming languages generally find
functional programming entertaining (and often enlightening), at least.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java compatibility issues (WAS: MF having issues?)

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-12T05:28:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XjOQf.120427$TK5.3479@fe06.news.easynews.com>`
