## Thread: Where I can Download Netexpress ? — 29 this month / 38 total

_Thread started [2006-02](2006-02.md#t-8d1c4df65f)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reasonably priced COBOL products

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2006-03-01T20:20:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dmnNf.83799$B94.15337@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1141185596.021397.148770@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1140619361.115449.221510@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <pK4Lf.61086$sa3.53288@pd7tw1no> <1140903840.317040.55330@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1eKMf.212521$AL5.135155@fe01.news.easynews.com> <1141185596.021397.148770@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
tsquare21 wrote:
> Good points Bill, I would have to agree that cobol except entriprise
> programing is not very viable solution for small development, cost the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> was on 3 disks, what a joke.

Firstly, if you trace back Bill's messages over the years, he has always 
expressed the opinion that it was not viable for PC COBOL vendors to 
produce 'cheap' compilers that would give them a steady revenue source. 
Dear old VISOC at about $800 a crack zoomed up to about $3,000 in Net 
Express V 1.0. Net Express V 3.1 was the one that introduced the 
universal runtime feature - but note there was a time gap until 3.1 - 
VISOC, N/E V 1, 2, 3.0 - by which time you had done a lot of coding 
using the new product !

To be fair to Micro Focus and the others - if you aren't manufacturing 
and selling big iron then you have to get 'innovative' for your revenue 
source. I think M/F have been overly 'innovative' to the point I for one 
have had it with the whole COBOL game. From a recent message I received, 
it isn't just the straight runtime fees - the author  quotes figures 
like the potential of $125,000 and $40,000 for using other M/F features !

> Yet COBOL, maybe the most EASIEST programing language to learn was
> never  developed to it's pointential.  All other Languages were
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> aware of it's limitation.

I'd be prepared to take a bet that COBOL is the EASIEST language to 
learn - and that was the original intention when it was designed, way 
back, in some six months. (I learned shorthand in the RAF - I can assure 
  you this message I'm writing is much EASIER to comprehend than 
shorthand - the only advantage of the latter is that you can quickly 
write down what is being said - and hope to God you can figure it out 
when you get back to typing it in your mother tongue !).

APIs, Local Variables, Graphic Engine. Go back to the late fifties/early 
sixties the mainframe vendors had the big stick to wield their clout. 
Hindsight, yes, but they could have controlled where the computer market 
went, but as a group completely missed the potential impact of the dinky 
toys - PC desktops. I don't want to put words in his mouth, but I 
believe while a member of the COBOL Standards (J4) Bill agreed with the 
long held general consensus - COBOL should NOT be designed to 
acknowledge individual operating systems. Fair enough in one sense. But 
given that thought, how could we slot in at least two of your points 
above - APIs and a Graphic Engine.

As you say there are workarounds - but the real solution becomes Java or 
C, which is where anybody, with still some 20 years of working life, 
will likely finish up.

When it comes to the 'neutrality' feature in COBOL there has always been 
politics to contend with. Way back when Grace Hopper was involved, Big 
Blue wanted to keep a certain feature 'confidential'. Grace and another 
didn't like that, and 'gently suggested' to the then Canadian 
representative on the Standards, that he should 'accidentally' release 
this particular document ! Confidentiality was blown.

My own impression - Big Blue is still a big offender. Look at some of 
the messages that get posted here, understanding JCL, SORTS, DB2 etc., 
not one COBOL RESERVED WORD in the whole bunch. They're interested in 
the big iron sales and you can use which of their languages you like. 
Reflect - what's dramatically different about using their languages 
dovetailed to their O/S, compared to Bill Gates and Windows ?

Absolutely no reflection on the lady who represents IBM on J4, (and 
Chuck Stevens can attest to her contribution, particularly as he is/has 
taken on her old job of chairing the ISO COBOL Committee), but 
regardless of her efforts, my feeling is that the real decisions are 
made much higher up above her in the IBM family tree. An example - I 
looked at an old paper on COBOL OO Collections which contained a phrase 
like 'the Base Class does not have to be in COBOL' - funny I said, why 
would you want to do that ? IBM Enterprise is released with skeleton OO 
classes, but NO collections and it reads like :-

'MyParticular Class inherits for JavaBase'

- Java is used to create/destroy objects and you do all your fancy GUI 
stuff and lists/Maps in Java !

Even where OO is included in COBOL it is treated as a second-league 
contender. J4, the ANSI Committee, is an 'American' committee which does 
welcome 'outsiders', but over time the situation has become that the ISO 
Committee determines what COBOL will have and pass that on to J4. So 
there are 'teams' from several countries represented on the ISO. (The 
American team is largely the J4 members). The others, fair bet they are 
all, mainly, from development companies being big iron users, (as the 
vendor market has all but disappeared in Europe), and just want to mosey 
along without getting involved in OO. (From a practical point of view, 
regarding ISO, how many small folks (PC Windows/Unix/Linux developers) 
could afford/waste the time or money to represent their countries).

Fairly recent emphasis has been on amending the following feature in 
COBOL :-

Working-Storage Tables 'OCCURS x TIMES' or "OCCURS x DEPENDING ON y"

Whichever way you cut the cookie you still finish up with a fixed 
allocation of memory, used or otherwise. The solution is there in OO 
where you can grow/increase/decrease Collection sizes at will. Nah ! 
Let's stick with the tried and true W/S Table and we'll build in a 
solution comparable to the OO approach.

I'm sure it is the EASI3EST LANGUAGE TO COMPREHEND
> So why don't I just move to another platform.  I like COBOL, as i said
> it's so easy to learn and program.  Personaly I just don't wan't to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
If you have years and years of COBOL coding, giving you some rock-solid 
libraries, I can certainly see your reticence to do a switch. Given that 
you have another 20 years work life, career wise, play it safe and get a 
handle on C or Java. Particularly so if you are US-based where we see 
occasional messages bitching about folks getting in with green cards or 
outsourcing. Your work competitors in Asia, (outsourcing), are growing 
and growing.

Meanwhile the whole COBOL game, Standards, vendors and developers are 
shooting COBOL in the foot - certainly developers sticking with a 
mindset of COBOL '85. Not 2010, 'cos that's just around the corner, but 
2020, a weakened COBOL market ? 2030 - COBOL might just be an 
interesting historical footnote ?

Interesting thought - given 2020 or 2030 I wonder what either C or Java 
will, look like ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reasonably priced COBOL products

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-03-01T13:51:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<br1c02l7890rnjfessg9glth1knd0snsr7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1140619361.115449.221510@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <pK4Lf.61086$sa3.53288@pd7tw1no> <1140903840.317040.55330@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1eKMf.212521$AL5.135155@fe01.news.easynews.com> <1141185596.021397.148770@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dmnNf.83799$B94.15337@pd7tw3no>`

```
On Wed, 01 Mar 2006 20:20:25 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

>Interesting thought - given 2020 or 2030 I wonder what either C or Java 
>will, look like ?

No idea.   But in 2030 programmers are going to learn what languages
don't cost them much money to learn.    Small businesses are going to
use languages that don't cost them much of an up-front investment.
Large businesses are going to use languages for which they can find
programmers that don't take expensive training.


Java isn't the most popular new language - HTML beats it.   But nobody
is predicting what HTML (or whatever replaces it) will be like in 20
years.    The need that HTML fulfilled wasn't seen 20 years ago - back
when we all knew the future of programming.
```

```
James J. Gavan<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> 03/01/06 1:20 PM >>>
>My own impression - Big Blue is still a big offender. Look at some of 
>the messages that get posted here, understanding JCL, SORTS, DB2 etc., 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>stuff and lists/Maps in Java !

Yeah, no offense to anyone intended (well, maybe), but IBM OO COBOL seems to
be pretty much a joke.  Take a look at the following restrictions:
"Restrictions:  
COBOL class definitions and methods cannot contain EXEC SQL statements and
cannot be compiled using the SQL compiler option. 
COBOL class definitions and methods cannot contain EXEC CICS statements,
cannot be run in a CICS environment, and cannot be compiled using the CICS
compiler option."

Crazy!  DB2 and CICS seems to be to just *beg* for OO capabilities.  I think
there were other seemingly silly restrictions, but I can't recall them at
the moment.

Up to this point I was pissed at IBM for not making COBOL/VSE OO.  But now
that I see this I can see that they simply spared me more aggravation!

Frank


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

```
Frank Swarbrick <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:
>James J. Gavan<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> 03/01/06 1:20 PM >>>

[snip]

>>- Java is used to create/destroy objects and you do all your fancy GUI 
>>stuff and lists/Maps in Java !
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Crazy!  DB2 and CICS seems to be to just *beg* for OO capabilities.

Mr Swarbrick, I never learned much about CICS internals... but I recall 
from someplace that the Customer Information Control System, in its 
earlier incarnations, was best described as 'a patch on the System370 
operating system'.  DB2 was, similarly, a way to access a mess ('mess' in 
both senses of 'disorderly' and 'a large number') of VSAM files by using 
SQL that had been interpreted into CALLs.

Getting into the midst of those twenty- and forty-year-old kludgestacks 
might not be the most attracting of alternatives.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reasonably priced COBOL products

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-04T22:18:47+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46t4bpFclaoiU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1140619361.115449.221510@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <pK4Lf.61086$sa3.53288@pd7tw1no> <1140903840.317040.55330@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1eKMf.212521$AL5.135155@fe01.news.easynews.com> <1141185596.021397.148770@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dmnNf.83799$B94.15337@pd7tw3no> <46s641Fcn41sU1@individual.net>`

```

"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
news:46s641Fcn41sU1@individual.net...
> James J. Gavan<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> 03/01/06 1:20 PM >>>
>>My own impression - Big Blue is still a big offender. Look at some of
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I share your disappointment, Frank.

But I can understand why this is really difficult for them. If you have many 
instances of an object running from the same terminal, all making demands on 
CICS, it becomes extremely difficult to attach terminal dependent buffers 
(DFHCOMM area). CICS is designed to have ONE thread from each terminal at a 
time. It multithreads the terminals, but making it multithread each 'leaf' 
of a node is probably a bigger investment than IBM are prepared to make at 
this time.

With SQL I suspect that the problem is sharing connections. The same problem 
arises; how do you know which instance of an object has the connection, in a 
system that is not designed to provide more than one connection to a given 
terminal? If the SQL was talking over ODBC it probably might not be so 
problematic (I'm not sure; although I seem to recall that you can only USE 
one connection at any given moment, even though you can have several named 
in your program...If the program is cloning instances of itself I can see 
how this might be problematic.

I would stress that all of the above is speculation; I don't really know. 
Whatever their reasons, it is a pity.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ IBM, OO COBOL and related IBM products(was: Reasonably priced COBOL products

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-04T13:07:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GigOf.297804$AL5.272332@fe01.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1140619361.115449.221510@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <pK4Lf.61086$sa3.53288@pd7tw1no> <1140903840.317040.55330@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1eKMf.212521$AL5.135155@fe01.news.easynews.com> <1141185596.021397.148770@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dmnNf.83799$B94.15337@pd7tw3no> <46s641Fcn41sU1@individual.net> <46t4bpFclaoiU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete, Frank, et al,
  Regarding IBM mainframe Object Oriented COBOL with CICS and/or DB2

Although IBM does not (currently) support EXEC CICS within OO methods, it DOES 
provide (for z/OS customers) documented/supported "OO, Java, CICS, COBOL 
interoperability"

For information, see (for example),

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dfhpjb02/5.1.1

 and

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dfhpjb02/5.8.1

   ***

As far as Object Oriented COBOL and DB2, see:

     http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dsnapj11/2.4.3.12


  ***

Having said this, Frank and I have previously had both public and private 
discussion on the state of IBM's continued support (via ENHANCEMENTS) for COBOL 
under VSE (or zVSE).  It may not be "non-existent" but it is close enough t to 
that to be almost indistinguishable.
```

```
First let me state that I have no direct experience with this, but from my 
reading you can write new CICS transactions in Java using an IBM package 
(that a library to COBOL folks) named JCICS. You can also access existing 
CICS transactions and you can mix COBOL and Java together within a 
transaction.  There are various restrictions depending on how things are set 
up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM, OO COBOL and related IBM products(was: Reasonably priced COBOL products

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "charles hottel" <jghottel@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-04T13:13:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4a4d$4409d8c5$4f9c662$12726@DIALUPUSA.NET>`
- **References:** `<1140619361.115449.221510@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <pK4Lf.61086$sa3.53288@pd7tw1no> <1140903840.317040.55330@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1eKMf.212521$AL5.135155@fe01.news.easynews.com> <1141185596.021397.148770@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dmnNf.83799$B94.15337@pd7tw3no> <46s641Fcn41sU1@individual.net> <46t4bpFclaoiU1@individual.net> <GigOf.297804$AL5.272332@fe01.news.easynews.com> <11ab8$4409d148$4f9c662$12434@DIALUPUSA.NET>`

```
I forgot to mention the book "Java for S/390 and AS/400 COBOL Programmers" 
discusses this. Even though it is a little dated and even though I have read 
several other 1000 page plus Java books I appreciated its business 
programming perspective.  It give Java equivalents to many COBOL verbs but 
still discusses Java ideas that have no direct COBOL equivalent.  It lists 
some of the JCICS objects and methods.  Unlike with other languages there is 
no Java CICS preprocessor, instead the functionality is provided via Java 
objects.

For example I posted a question here a long while back about scrolling 
forward and backward while displaying records from a DB2 database. I wanted 
to limit the number of records read from the database. IIRC the answers I 
received were all about DB2 SQL parameters I could use. This book mentioned 
mentioned a Java method named setMaxRows in several Java classes (e.g. 
CallableStatement, PreparedStatement, Statement, etc.) which addresses this 
function.  None of the other books I have read mentioned this method and I 
think these authors did mention it because of their business applications 
perspective.

> reading you can write new CICS transactions in Java using an IBM package
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reasonably priced COBOL products

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-05T16:36:54+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46v4mqFcq1k3U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1140619361.115449.221510@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <46s641Fcn41sU1@individual.net> <46t4bpFclaoiU1@individual.net> <1141484334.825117.206340@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1141484334.825117.206340@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> standard OO Cobol book wrong?).

No, Alastair, your book is not wrong. Each instance of an object IS 
identifiable by the system. But CICS (I think) is interested in which 
TERMINAL a thread emanates from. (The DFHCOMM area is TERMINAL dependant, 
just like the SPA in IMS.) This is a teleprocesssing consideration, not a 
system task consideration (like the running of instances of code). In an OO 
environment you could have multiple instances of an object running from the 
same terminal. While the system can identify (and assign local storage etc 
to) these instances, there is no guarantee that the TP monitor can.

I would stress that it is many years since I worked in a CICS environment 
and my comments are entirely speculative, based on system programming 
experience gained on various platforms years ago... It could be completely 
different now. However, I am fairly certain that it would be non-trivial to 
add this support to OO COBOL. If many sites embraced OO there would be some 
impetus to do it, but with the situation the way it is, it would be hard to 
make a business case for the considerable spend involved.

BOTTOM LINE: I'm not surprised that OO COBOL is the 'poor relation' in the 
IBM world.


> Otherwise, your suppositions/hypotheses
> seem reasonable to me (ignorant fool that I am).

:-) You are in real trouble when what I write here starts sounding 
reasonable...


> However, perhaps there
```

- **References:** `<1140619361.115449.221510@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <46s641Fcn41sU1@individual.net> <46t4bpFclaoiU1@individual.net> <1141484334.825117.206340@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Alistair<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> 03/04/06 7:58 AM >>>
>
>Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> But I can understand why this is really difficult for them. If you have
many
>> instances of an object running from the same terminal, all making demands
on
>> CICS, it becomes extremely difficult to attach terminal dependent
buffers
>> (DFHCOMM area). CICS is designed to have ONE thread from each terminal at
a
>> time. It multithreads the terminals, but making it multithread each
'leaf'
>> of a node is probably a bigger investment than IBM are prepared to make
at
>> this time.
>>
>> With SQL I suspect that the problem is sharing connections. The same
problem
>> arises; how do you know which instance of an object has the connection,
in a
>> system that is not designed to provide more than one connection to a
given
>> terminal?
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>which might not have been brought up to OO readiness?

Putting aside multithreading, I don't see why an OO COBOL program could not
run under CICS.  Each time you create an instance of a class it's not like
you're spawning a new program.  All you're doing is creating a new "data"
instance.  All of the methods that act upon the data are only in once place
in memory.  This is pretty much how CICS works now.  When a program is
initiated, CICS looks to see if the program is already loaded in memory.  If
it is, it simply gets memory for all of the working-storage for that program
and assigns it to the new task.  But the code itself is only in memory once
per CICS region.  I don't see that a new instance of an object of a class
(!) is really *all* that much different than using dynamic memory allocation
to create new data areas.  In other words, how is:

INVOKE class-name NEW . . . RETURNING object-reference1
INVOKE class-name NEW . . . RETURNING object-reference2
INVOKE class-name NEW . . . RETURNING object-reference3

much different, in terms of using memory, than

CALL CEEGTST USING HEAPID 
        BY CONTENT LENGTH OF DATA-AREA
        BY REFERENCE ADDRESS OF DATA-AREA1    
                        FC                     

CALL CEEGTST USING HEAPID 
        BY CONTENT LENGTH OF DATA-AREA
        BY REFERENCE ADDRESS OF DATA-AREA2    
                        FC                     

CALL CEEGTST USING HEAPID 
        BY CONTENT LENGTH OF DATA-AREA
        BY REFERENCE ADDRESS OF DATA-AREA3    
                        FC                     


(CEEGTST means Get Storage; similar to C's malloc())

Now as for the preprocessor issue, you may well be on to something there. 
But it's not much of an excuse!  Enterprise COBOL apparently now
incorporates those pre-processors into the compiler itself, so it seems to
me it should be even less of an issue.

Frank




--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reasonably priced COBOL products

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2006-03-07T05:07:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ny8Pf.113824$H%4.25279@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4746heFdemb1U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1140619361.115449.221510@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <46s641Fcn41sU1@individual.net> <46t4bpFclaoiU1@individual.net> <1141484334.825117.206340@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <4746heFdemb1U1@individual.net>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> Putting aside multithreading, I don't see why an OO COBOL program could not
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> me it should be even less of an issue.

I was reluctant to comment on this before, when I saw Alistair's 
message. So consider me a newbie to SQL, and I don't touch 
multi-threading or multi-user. Might just possibly give you some ideas. 
In Windows I'm using MS Access.

I need to access two DBs :-

Generic.mdb - contains common tables applicable to all clients
Facility.mdb - tables only relevant to a particular Location (oil 
company/facility).

For each table in the respective DBs I have a separate class per table - 
the only place SQL Statements appear. So when I do invoke "new", in 
effect, I have a 'singleton instance' per table.

At the current time, upfront, when the application starts, I invoke 
SqlConnect "new" returning os-SqlConnect - so just the one class 
instance handling CONNECTS. As Tables are initialized and used the 
object reference for os-SqlConnect is passed to each. (For frequently 
used tables I do this initialization at the same time as the initial 
SqlConnect setup).

The two DBs above are identified as Server 1 or Server 2. Hard-coded 
into each of the Tables referenced is a code denoting whether the 
particular table class belongs to Server 1 or Server 2, (CONSTANT - a 
Level 78 in M/F). Each method in a table starts :-

        initialize lnk-values
        invoke os-SqlConnection "setCurrentConnection"
               using ws-dbID returning lnk-SqlResult

        if TableError
           EXIT METHOD
        End-if

ws-dbID, (that CONSTANT above), is the value for Server 1 or 2 for this 
particular table.  Within os-SqlConnection  the code is :-

          initialize lnk-Result, ws-ErrorParams, ws-Result

          Evaluate true
            when lnk-id = ws-CurrentConnection
                 continue
            when lnk-id = 1
                  move lnk-ID to ws-CurrentConnection
                  EXEC SQL CONNECT TO :Server1 END-EXEC

            when other
                  move lnk-ID to ws-CurrentConnection
                  EXEC SQL CONNECT TO :Server2 END-EXEC

          End-evaluate

I'm inclined to make classes inherit for convenience, so I can use 
invoke "super" rather than invoke SqlConnectionClass..... This time I 
didn't. I've created a singleton instance of SqlConnection and the 
common object is being used by all DBs and tables.

It's decades ago since I ever did multi-user, and apart from which I 
don't have the equipment to test it. Whether taking a similar approach, 
where one single instance is used to control all CONNECTIONS and whether 
it would work in your environment - only you could know.

Jimmy
```

```
I do not know how hard it would be to implement thread support in CICS for 
COBOL programs, but there is nothing inherent in CICS to prevent it.  CICS 
has support for at least 3 flavors of Java Virtual Machine for running 
interpreted Java programs under CICS. There is an IBM Redbook that discusses 
this. I have only read part of it but I do not recall any multithreading 
restrictions.
There is also a Java compiler and IIRC it is called the Java High 
Performance (JHP) compiler.
```

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>

- **References:** `<1140619361.115449.221510@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <pK4Lf.61086$sa3.53288@pd7tw1no> <1140903840.317040.55330@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1eKMf.212521$AL5.135155@fe01.news.easynews.com> <1141185596.021397.148770@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dmnNf.83799$B94.15337@pd7tw3no> <46s641Fcn41sU1@individual.net> <46t4bpFclaoiU1@individual.net> <17a70$440afa30$4f9c64f$27051@DIALUPUSA.NET>`

```


charles hottel wrote:
> I do not know how hard it would be to implement thread support in CICS for 
> COBOL programs, but there is nothing inherent in CICS to prevent it.  CICS 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> restrictions.

I am certainly not an expert on this, but my understanding is that 
originally CICS ran as a single task (whether as a "batch" job or a 
started task).  This became a performance limitation.  Eventually, 
"VSAM subtasking" was added, which meant that a single CICS region 
could run at least two TCB's (Task Control Blocks), which speeded up 
performance quite a bit.

CICS application programs ran under the QRCB, the Quasi-Re-entrant 
Task Control Block.  Newer releases of CICS/Transaction Server support 
multiple TCB's, but you need to write your CICS COBOL programs so they 
are "threadsafe" in order to use multiple TCB's.  I think there's a 
redbook or other document that tells you how to do that.  Depending on 
what your existing CICS COBOL program does, it may be anywhere from 
fairly easy to almost impossible to rewite your program to be 
threadsafe.  The primary application I work with uses many 
long-running, conversational tasks with pseudoterminals.  It appears 
we will have difficulties if we try to make our CICS COBOL programs 
threadsafe.  This was not foreseen when the application was orignally 
designed in the late 1980's!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reasonably priced COBOL products

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-06T15:09:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ugYOf.263371$K35.135591@fe05.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1140619361.115449.221510@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <pK4Lf.61086$sa3.53288@pd7tw1no> <1140903840.317040.55330@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1eKMf.212521$AL5.135155@fe01.news.easynews.com> <1141185596.021397.148770@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dmnNf.83799$B94.15337@pd7tw3no> <46s641Fcn41sU1@individual.net> <46t4bpFclaoiU1@individual.net> <17a70$440afa30$4f9c64f$27051@DIALUPUSA.NET> <qaQOf.510840$qk4.271125@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
I'm not going to go into the "details" here (or even post URL references), but 
to summarize

1) Current IBM Enterprise COBOL has a THREAD compiler option.  I don't recall 
their being a restriction on its use with CICS

2) CICS TS (at least V2 and later) has a concept called "THREADSAFE" - this has 
NOTHING to do with current COBOL or LE thread options/terminology.  I have 
worked with Hursley a bit on this, and no one is REAL clear on how this does and 
doesn't work with COBOL/LE.  It is known that the current COBOL DISPLAY (upon a 
TD queue) is *NOT* threadsafe, but that most (all?) other COBOL syntax is.

3) Current CICS TS allows for COBOL programs compiled with the "IS RECURSIVE" 
syntax, but people I have talked to are NOT exactly certain how this really 
works (particularly with the LE CBLPSHPOP run-time option).

   ***

Bottom-Line:
  If you currently want to create a CICS TS (sorry about this Frank, I doubt how 
much of this is true for VSE) it is possible (I think) to create threaded and 
threadsafe applications.  However, this probably take some research and LOTS of 
testing.
```

```
>much of this is true for VSE) it is possible (I think) to create threaded
and

LOTS of 
>testing.


From
http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/IGY3PG30/2.4.52?SHELF=
igy3sh31&DT=20050628164603

"Although threaded applications are subject to a number of programming and
environment restrictions, the use of a program in nonthreaded applications
is not so restricted. For example, a program compiled with the THREAD option
can run in the CICS and IMS environments, can run AMODE 24, and can call and
be called by other programs that are not enabled for multithreading, as long
as the application does not contain multiple POSIX threads or PL/I tasks at
run time." 

I may well be reading this wrong, but this says to me that you can use the
THREAD compiler option for CICS programs, but when running under CICS they
*may use only a single (main) thread*!

Huh!

Frank


---
```

```
and

CICS

at

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>But I can understand why this is really difficult for them. If you have
many 
>instances of an object running from the same terminal, all making demands
on 
>CICS, it becomes extremely difficult to attach terminal dependent buffers 
>(DFHCOMM area). CICS is designed to have ONE thread from each terminal at a

>time. It multithreads the terminals, but making it multithread each 'leaf'

>of a node is probably a bigger investment than IBM are prepared to make at

>this time.
>
>With SQL I suspect that the problem is sharing connections. The same
problem 
>arises; how do you know which instance of an object has the connection, in
a 
>system that is not designed to provide more than one connection to a given

>terminal? If the SQL was talking over ODBC it probably might not be so 
>problematic (I'm not sure; although I seem to recall that you can only USE

>one connection at any given moment, even though you can have several named

>in your program...If the program is cloning instances of itself I can see 
>how this might be problematic.
>
>I would stress that all of the above is speculation; I don't really know. 
>Whatever their reasons, it is a pity.

Actually, I think that COBOL (and Java?) threads are disallowed under CICS
as well, so I'm not sure that is their reason.  But I don't really know...

As for DB2 connections, I *believe* that the CICS region itself owns the
connection(s), so I'm not sure that's an issue either.

Or I could be wrong on all counts!  I just know that the restrictions sound
crazy and, without explanation of why, rather arbitrary.

Frank
```

- **References:** `<1140619361.115449.221510@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <pK4Lf.61086$sa3.53288@pd7tw1no> <1140903840.317040.55330@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1eKMf.212521$AL5.135155@fe01.news.easynews.com> <1141185596.021397.148770@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
"tsquare21" <tlthomas21@netscape.com> wrote:

>Good points Bill, I would have to agree that cobol except entriprise
>programing is not very viable solution for small development, cost the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>was on 3 disks, what a joke.

We, even as a small company, have no major complaints about paying a
relatively substantial price for a good COBOL compiler, and as we're
in the UK we get "shafted" by the distinctly unfavourable Dollar to
Pound Sterling conversion that our American cousins like to foist on
their export prices... For example, the last MF compiler that we
bought (for PC-based work) was somewhere around UKP 3,000, so near
enough $5000. That's fine, a good compiler is a major piece of work,
it's not aimed at the "hobbyist" market, and the stuff that we've had
from MF has always worked very well and generated tight and
well-performing code.

Our problem is with runtime fees. We operate where the market sets the
price, and if that price is actually *less* than the runtime fee per
installation, we're screwed before we start. Game over.
```

---

```
Mike, V2.1 of NetCOBOL for .NET will not work with VS2005. V3.0 of
NetCOBOL for .NET is due out (I heard sometime around late first half
to early second half this year) this year and will work with VS2005.
```

```
                                COBOL FAQ
                      (Frequently Asked Questions)
    
    Last-Modified: Spring 2002

		http://home.comcast.net/~wmklein/FAQ/COBOLFAQ.htm 
   
    If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, 
    please send private email to:
    
        wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
   
   
    This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):
   
     - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
     - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
     - Where can I contact... ?
     - What about OO COBOL? 
     - Books about COBOL
     - COBOL Tools
     - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
     - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
     - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education? 
 		Tutorials? Etc
   
   	 and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: Link Fujitsu with ODBC — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Link Fujitsu with ODBC

- **From:** "Servizio Tecnico" <software(TOGLIQUESTO)@gescart.it>
- **Date:** 2006-03-02T12:20:35+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4406d4f7$0$18299$4fafbaef@reader1.news.tin.it>`

```
Hello,

I must link with embedded SQL one file .MDB. Can I use DNS-less function?

Now I use this:
     exec sql
         connect to 'FuturaPJ' user 'Administrator/.'
     end-exec.
where:

FUTURAPJ = Link created with ODBC in Control Panel and written in a INF file 
described in COBOL85.CBR:

@ODBC_Inf=Futura.Inf

Where inside Futura.Inf:

[FuturaPJ]
@SQL_DATASRC=FuturaPJ

In VB, for example, DNS-less function is possible. Is possible inFujitsu v7 
too?

Thanks in advance.
Roberto
```

```
As Fujistu COBOL is limited on what you can use on their ESQL I do not

plain ADO.

with Fujitsu NETCOBOL" for an alternative way.
there is a learning curve required, but at the end it will probably be
```

<a id="t-fa01bcfc71"></a>
## Thread: [OT] As Was Done With Training, Perhaps — 26 msgs

### [OT] As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-03-02T15:34:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

There have been, at times, discussions here about how many employers train 
(or, more frequently, don't train) employees to deal with changes in the 
technologies involved with their jobs... how a company seeking to 
implement web-based functions will hire new Java-jockies instead of 
sending their COBOL-coders to classes, for example.  I've often thought 
that reasoning was bipartite, but with a single basis:

1) Sending someone to class Costs Money.

2) Once the person has completed the class they may be hired away by a 
competitor... so to keep that person on the job a pay-raise might need to 
be given and this Costs Money.

Anyhow... I was catching up on a bit of reading and came across an article 
in the 18 - 24 Feb 2006 issue of The Economist, a 'special report' on 
dealing with the ageing workforce, entitled 'Turning Boomers into 
Boomerangs'.  (A 'boomer' is someone born during the post-WWII 'baby boom' 
and a 'boomerang' is someone who returns to work after an absence).  I was 
struck by the following:

--begin quoted text:

A survey in America last month by Ernst & Young found that "although 
corporate America foresees a significant workforce shortage as boomers 
retire, it is not dealing with the issue." Almost three-quarters of the 
1,400 global companies questioned by Deloitte last year said they expected 
a shortage of salaried staff over the next three to five years. Yet few of 
them are looking to older workers to fill that shortage; and even fewer 
are looking to them to fill another gap that has already appeared. Many 
firms in Europe and America complain that they struggle to find qualified 
directors for their boards�this when the pool of retired talent from those 
very same firms is growing by leaps and bounds.

Why are firms not working harder to keep old employees? Part of the reason 
is that the crunch has been beyond the horizon of most managers. Nor is 
hanging on to older workers the only way to cope with a falling supply of 
labour. The participation of developing countries in the world economy has 
increased the overall supply�whatever the local effect of demographics in 
the rich countries. A vast amount of work is being sent offshore to such 
places as China and India and more will go in future. Some countries, such 
as Australia, are relaxing their immigration policies to allow much needed 
skills to come in from abroad. Others will avoid the need for workers by 
spending money on machinery and automation.

--end quoted text

... and then there's, of course, a simple reason... older, more 
experienced workers might have a better knowledge of the value their work 
provides an organisation and might, as a result, demand higher wages... 
and this Costs Money.  

Never mind that it might take two recent college grads thrice the time it 
takes someone with thirty-odd years' worth of experience to accomplish a 
task... the two grads' salaries, combined, are - on a quarterly basis - 
less than the geezer's.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-04T00:41:25+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46qob8Fcgl0iU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com...
>
> There have been, at times, discussions here about how many employers train
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> sending their COBOL-coders to classes, for example.

This depends entirely on the attitude of the said COBOL-coders. If they 
think they have nothing to learn, it is simply a waste of corporate cash to 
train them. Unfortunately, there are those who regard everything new as a 
re-invention of what they already know. Would you spend your money to train 
such people? Of course, there are also those who genuinely DO want to learn, 
but this is not a good investment from the company point of view, unless the 
person concerned can show that they are worth the investment and will return 
on it. ( a young grad has potentially 30 years to offer the company; a 
geezer has maybe 5 or 6, maximum 10. I don't personally agree with this, 
because I realise that people can leave anytime, irrespective of their age, 
but it is the perceived wisdom.).  Someone who had got stuck into Java in 
his own time and could show some applets or similar running, would have a 
lot more credibility than someone who just wanted some training, with a view 
to leaving as soon as he has a certificate. Throughout my career I have 
never relied on a company to provide training. Knowledge is much too 
important (and valuable) to let someone else decide whether you get it or 
not.

> I've often thought
> that reasoning was bipartite, but with a single basis:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have known many short sighted employers who saw it this way; fortunately 
there are also many who don't.

Realistic employers know that WHATEVER they do it is going to cost money. 
Better to invest the money in their staff, value their staff (and be seen to 
do so), have an active social program and put money into it, and make sure 
the people have an incentive to come to work, than to pay more money for 
someone external. People don't get hired away on completion of a class, if 
that class is part of a three year plan for personal growth and subsidised 
education. (I have a couple of people on my team at the moment who are glad 
to utilise such opportunites...) There is always a chance they will leave, 
because there is always a chance ANYONE can leave ANYTIME, but my own 
assessment is that they appreciate the help and will repay it with loyalty.

(Just as a matter of interest, and to illustrate how management attitude can 
generate a good culture, here is a description of what permanent employees 
of this company can expect (I have not disclosed  details of the 
confidential perquisites like performance related bonuses, share options and 
investment, which are not just offered to senior executives but to all 
employees...):

1. An ultra modern  building (it has been open for just over 2 months) in 
the centre of the Auckland CBD (Commercial Business District, the heart of 
the City) with an open plan office that has views over Auckland harbour and 
the North Shore.

2. There is a relaxation area with free coffee, tea, etc and refrigerators, 
microwaves, and dishwashers for staff use. The same area has a (free) pool 
table which can be used at any time of day. The latest copies of magazines 
and newspapers are in this area, and staff are encouraged to use it at any 
time.  It is furnished like a lounge with coffee tables and easy chairs and 
sofas.

3. Instead of managers having separate offices, they sit with the staff in 
the open plan area. There is no difference between my desk (or my Boss's 
desk) and anyone else's... For confidential discussions, there are "quiet 
rooms" always available.

4. Presentation rooms are available on each of the floors we occupy. (each 
of the other floors is laid out just as described.) These rooms are equipped 
with widescreen TVs connected to wireless internet and you can do your 
presentation with a wireless keyboard and wireless mouse. Video conferencing 
is available and used quite a lot, especially to Australia.

It is a "salubrious" environment and care has been taken to make it 
attractive  (around 2 million dollars was spent on artwork for the 
building - this is an Auckland City Council requirement: 1% of the building 
value...), and a place that people would want to come to work.

Of course, buildings and toys are one thing, and not every company 
(especially small ones) can afford these things, but the workplace "culture" 
is much more important. Just one example to demonstrate...

Recently there was some concern about the social club. Social club funds 
were low and activities that were being planned might not be viable unless 
more people joined up. The committe met with the management. Management have 
always said they valued the club's activities (quizzes, trips, parties, 
etc.) and so it was decided that rather than people being excluded from 
these activities because they hadn't joined the social club, the company 
would simply pay everyone's subscription so that all staff (including 
contract) could participate in the activities. This is a perfect example of 
management investing (substantially) in their staff. (Of course the 
management will also enjoy the outings and activities, but that is how it 
should be.) The result is that the social club can now plan an active 
calendar throughout the year, instead of just a Christmas party...


> Anyhow... I was catching up on a bit of reading and came across an article
> in the 18 - 24 Feb 2006 issue of The Economist, a 'special report' on
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Was it a boomerang :-)?

> --begin quoted text:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, The Economist is always a bit suspect in my view (much too LSE if you 
ask me... :-)) and the point about Australia is certainly not the perceived 
policy. Australia has one of the tightest immigration policies in the world. 
Our's is too slack; their's is too tight. Possibly both countries are moving 
towards correction of the situation.

> ... and then there's, of course, a simple reason... older, more
> experienced workers might have a better knowledge of the value their work
> provides an organisation and might, as a result, demand higher wages...
> and this Costs Money.
>
The man gave three reasons that contribute to this situation, but you see 
only one, and a cynical one at that :-)


> Never mind that it might take two recent college grads thrice the time it
> takes someone with thirty-odd years' worth of experience to accomplish a
> task... the two grads' salaries, combined, are - on a quarterly basis -
> less than the geezer's.
>
Doc, I really hope the cynicism you express is limited to CLC and not 
propagated to your colleagues... It would make for a gloomy workplace. 
(Well, it would for the geezers... :-))

Anyone, whether they're old or young cannot price themself off the market. 
If you demand more than the job is worth don't be surprised if you're 
unemployed for lengthy periods.(Ooops, I forgot... you have that one 
covered... :-))

Sure, there will be grads getting jobs that geezers might have done, but 
that has always been the way of the world. Young people are an exploited 
resource in many places, but that is changing too.

I can honestly say I have never felt discriminated against because of my 
age. (It would be illegal in NZ anyway; you cannot even ask someone how old 
they are in an interview, and people do NOT have to put their date of birth 
on their CV. Neither can you ask them their religion or their sexual 
preferences (maybe you can if the job is in the sex industry :-), not sure 
about that one...), it all works pretty well.

I got the contract I am currently doing in the face of fierce competition 
from dozens of applicants, most of whom were younger than me. I didn't drop 
the rate so I have to assume they decided I was right for the job, based on 
track record, references, and interview impression. (I had never met any of 
the people who interviewed me and there was no "old boy" network involved. 
I'd have more chance of that in Europe where I actually know people , than 
in NZ where I know nobody after an absence from the IT scene of around 30 
years...)

Maybe attitude has a bearing on it? I don't think of myself as a geezer and 
I am passionate about what I do. It comes across in interviews. Maybe the 
opposite does too...?

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-03-03T07:17:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0njg02d6n2fjkperb8kntur36uhieinrfi@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com> <46qob8Fcgl0iU1@individual.net>`

```
On Sat, 4 Mar 2006 00:41:25 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Realistic employers know that WHATEVER they do it is going to cost money. 
>Better to invest the money in their staff, value their staff (and be seen to 
>do so), have an active social program and put money into it, and make sure 
>the people have an incentive to come to work, than to pay more money for 
>someone external.

Managers know they will need more managers - managers don't know that
they will need XML programmers.   So train CoBOL programmers to become
managers - reward them by allowing them to become one of us.   If they
don't want to join the superior set, then it's their own fault.

If that doesn't work, wait until you are sure that you will be using a
technology long enough to justify training.   After getting the new
system, and learning what is needed for a few years is a good time. Of
course, the old CoBOL programmers will be low man on the totem pole
behind the kids you hired to do this job while you were deciding. But
that's OK, if they were any good, they would have seen the writing on
the wall and switched jobs.    Anyway, we can't worry about that now,
as it's time to work on the next system which will replace the one we
just got - and that's management work.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

```
In article <46qob8Fcgl0iU1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>This depends entirely on the attitude of the said COBOL-coders.

Everything depends on something else, Mr Dashwood... including this 
statement.

>If they 
>think they have nothing to learn, it is simply a waste of corporate cash to 
>train them. Unfortunately, there are those who regard everything new as a 
>re-invention of what they already know. Would you spend your money to train 
>such people?

I would not have hired them in the first place... but the attitudes of 
employee might just, possibly, be a reflection of the attitudes of the 
organisation which employs them; the behaviors which are rewarded are 
those which are most likely to be propogated.

[snip]

>Someone who had got stuck into Java in 
>his own time and could show some applets or similar running, would have a 
>lot more credibility than someone who just wanted some training, with a view 
>to leaving as soon as he has a certificate.

One might wish to consider the reasons that such a person might have for 
leaving, Mr Dashwood.  Granted that I barely know why *I* do things, let 
alone anyone else... but if one is employed by an organisation that 
invests in (trains) employees and then allows them to participate in the 
rewards which such investments demonstrate (do well in the training, get a 
higher salary) then there might be fewer post-training defections.

>Throughout my career I have 
>never relied on a company to provide training. Knowledge is much too 
>important (and valuable) to let someone else decide whether you get it or 
>not.

On the other hand... having knowledgable, well-trained, motivated 
employees is in the organisation's own best interest.  If an organisation 
is not willing or able to take steps to act in its own best interest then 
it might be reasonable to conclude that it will not act in its employees' 
best interests, either... and an organisation that will not act in my best 
interest might be one that I would seek to separate myself from posthaste.

>
>> I've often thought
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>someone external.

It is my experience, Mr Dashwood, that 'realistic employers' are few and 
far between.

>People don't get hired away on completion of a class, if 
>that class is part of a three year plan for personal growth and subsidised 
>education.

Bingo... see above about 'mutual best interests'.

[snip]

>> Anyhow... I was catching up on a bit of reading and came across an article
>> in the 18 - 24 Feb 2006 issue of The Economist, a 'special report' on
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>policy. Australia has one of the tightest immigration policies in the world. 

That might be changing, Mr Dashwood... from a possibly-suspect source, 
http://www.scoop.co.nz/stories/WO0603/S00072.htm

--begin quoted text:

Australian Greens Senator Kerry Nettle today called on the Government to 
implement the recommendations of the Senate Inquiry into the Migration Act 
tabled today, and seriously consider abandoning the policy of mandatory 
detention.

[snip]

"The Inquiry found the immigration system is inconsistent and treats 
people without humanity - the system is broken."

--end quoted text

>Our's is too slack; their's is too tight. Possibly both countries are moving 
>towards correction of the situation.

Exactly... and if Australia is 'moving towards correction of the 
situation' then The Economist's assertion that '(Australia is) relaxing 
their immigration policies to allow much needed skills to come in from 
abroad' might be seen as true.

>
>> ... and then there's, of course, a simple reason... older, more
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>only one, and a cynical one at that :-)

Considering costs, when it comes to business, is 'cynical', Mr Dashwood?  
That will be interesting news to those who draw up ROI and CBA papers.

>
>> Never mind that it might take two recent college grads thrice the time it
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>(Well, it would for the geezers... :-))

I am, on most of my consulting sites, the 'geezer'... the one who is 
willing to say 'How was this situation addressed in the days of 
card-sorts?' and 'Let's not rush into this... a good hunter watches and 
lines things up so that one shot is needed.'

>Maybe attitude has a bearing on it? I don't think of myself as a geezer and 
>I am passionate about what I do. It comes across in interviews. Maybe the 
>opposite does too...?

I do my best to combine the two, Mr Dashwood... the ability to watch and 
wait for the appropriate moment, the Anciente Knowledge of the Oldene 
Dayse... and then the fierce, no-holds-barred 'Let's Do It!' enthusiasm of 
my Kollidj Kid days.  Sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't... but 
that's true of any statement, including this one.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-05T00:38:07+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46tch1Fcdsi2U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com> <46qob8Fcgl0iU1@individual.net> <du9lu3$ss6$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
A good response Doc. I don't think you are as cranky as you pretend.:-)

Both you and Howard seem to have a low opinion of management and their 
motivation.

Having worked with bad management (as well as good), and tried to emulate 
and learn from the latter it, is very possible that my view is too 
optimistic. However, it is my view.

I do believe (and have expressed it here before), that all of us have some 
responsibility for the people who manage us. We can influence them, and we 
can simply refuse to accept bad management. (There is no excuse for 
rudeness, bullying, improper use of power, or inappropriate behaviour, for 
example).

But stupidity is something else. Management don't have a monopoly on it. The 
best projects work as teams, with management simply administrating, guiding 
and monitoring progress. Project (work) decisions should be discussed by the 
team and  implemented with the approval of the majority, after due debate 
with the ones who disagree. None of us is as smart as all of us.

I've decided not to argue this any more (you may consider the motion that 
'Management sucks...' carried :-)) because it has to be subjective, and it 
has to depend on variables like corporate culture and the location where you 
are working.

I simply hope you find better managers. :-)

Pete.












<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:du9lu3$ss6$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <46qob8Fcgl0iU1@individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[210 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-04T08:58:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<120jaois31dh62e@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com> <46qob8Fcgl0iU1@individual.net> <du9lu3$ss6$1@reader2.panix.com> <46tch1Fcdsi2U1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> A good response Doc. I don't think you are as cranky as you
> pretend.:-)
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> inappropriate behaviour, for example).

Hear, hear!

I once got a new manager. He started off by being rude, officious, and 
profane. After a couple of days, I went to his office and said, inter alia, 
"We've gotten off on the wrong foot. I suggest we start over and act in a 
professional, courteous manner. I, for my part, am willing and eager to do 
blah-blah-blah. On the other hand, if you ever again call me a 'stupid 
son-of-a-bitch' in front of other employees, I will beat the shit out of 
you."

He fired me the next day.

That evening, I caught him in the parking lot and beat the shit out of him.

I got a visit from the law. Inasmuch as I was also a reserve deputy sheriff, 
the investigating officer told my ex-manager to shut up or expect another 
beating.

Inconvenient, but it worked out in the end, as most things do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-05T14:35:27+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46utj2Fcvie6U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com> <46qob8Fcgl0iU1@individual.net> <du9lu3$ss6$1@reader2.panix.com> <46tch1Fcdsi2U1@individual.net> <120jaois31dh62e@news.supernews.com>`

```

"HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:120jaois31dh62e@news.supernews.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> A good response Doc. I don't think you are as cranky as you
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think that is very fair... :-)  As a manager, I would never be in that 
situation in the first place because it is simple common courtesy not to 
abuse or denigrate staff in public, and especially not in front of 
colleagues. The man had fair warning.



> He fired me the next day.
>

Alas, this just demonstrates the position that has been established here 
previously: Management sucks. This person was obviously totally incapable of 
managing people and you have to wonder how he ever got the job.


> That evening, I caught him in the parking lot and beat the shit out of 
> him.
>
:-)  While I can't condone that as a solution, I can certainly understand 
how and why it happened. There is a justice here, and, given that it wasn't 
a cowardly sneak attack, I would agree that he had it coming. Besides, it is 
very important to do what you say you are going to do.

In my entire career I have had one occasion where I hit somebody. (It wasn't 
a fist fight because he didn't get up, andI don't attack peope on the 
ground). It was not in the workplace, I had extreme provocation and had 
given two warnings. I'm not proud of it and I don't recommend it as standard 
practice for Project Managers :-). I was fairly certain the person cncerned 
would get me fired for it, but instead, the next morning he came to my 
office, agreed he had deserved it, and suggested both of us forget it and 
move on. We did and there were no further problems after that.



> I got a visit from the law. Inasmuch as I was also a reserve deputy 
> sheriff, the investigating officer told my ex-manager to shut up or expect 
> another beating.
>
:-)

> Inconvenient, but it worked out in the end, as most things do.
>

Sure. What goes around comes around...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-03-05T13:50:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dueqbi$fqg$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com> <du9lu3$ss6$1@reader2.panix.com> <46tch1Fcdsi2U1@individual.net> <120jaois31dh62e@news.supernews.com>`

```
In article <120jaois31dh62e@news.supernews.com>,
HeyBub <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com> wrote:
>Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> A good response Doc. I don't think you are as cranky as you
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>you."

My technique for dealing with such activities is a bit different; I try to 
avoid the 'abuse in public, apologise in private' syndrome that the above 
might lead to.

Anyone: 'Well, it's *got* to be WABBA YABBA BLOOGA, YOU (expletive)!'

Me:  'Mr/Ms Smith, there is no need to raise your voice to me/address me 
in that manner.'

Anyone: 'Sorry... I just get... enthusiastic, sometimes... but it's just 
*got* to be WABBA YABBA BLOOGA, YOU (expletive)!'

Me: 'Ms/Mr Smith, I will not participate in being addressed in that 
manner, if it happens again I will leave.'

Anyone:  'Hey, I don't see what's wrong, I say that to everyone... and 
it's *got* to be WABBA BABBA... HEY!  WHERE THE (expletive) ARE YOU 
GOING?'

Me:  'I'm going to Mr/Ms Jones' office to explain that I am quitting, 
*now*, because you insist on addressing me in a fashion which establishes 
a hostile work environment.'

(note: in the USA an employer's permitting of a 'hostile work environment' 
has been the source of Very Large Legal Settlements)

I then find myself another contract.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>>
>> Hear, hear!
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Good advice. My episode took place before "hostile work environments" 
existed as a legal cause. Also, I'm in Texas where we have a culture of 
"taking steps." As a sheriff once said after being taunted by a tricky 
defense attorney outside the courthouse in Houston (and in front of dozens 
of reporters): "You better quit fuckin' with me. I can get you killed for a 
case of beer!"

I, being out of a job at the time, didn't want to spring for a case of beer. 
So I dealt with the matter personally.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-03-04T15:04:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<duca9l$h1l$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com> <46qob8Fcgl0iU1@individual.net> <du9lu3$ss6$1@reader2.panix.com> <46tch1Fcdsi2U1@individual.net>`

```
In article <46tch1Fcdsi2U1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>A good response Doc. I don't think you are as cranky as you pretend.:-)
>
>Both you and Howard seem to have a low opinion of management and their 
>motivation.

Mr Dashwood, I cannot speak for Mr Brazee... but my views are based on my 
experiences and I have pointed out before that these experiences have 
shown me a contractor/consultant/hired gun sees the insides of 'sick 
shops' more often than healthy ones.

>
>Having worked with bad management (as well as good), and tried to emulate 
>and learn from the latter it, is very possible that my view is too 
>optimistic. However, it is my view.

If I wanted only agreement, Mr Dashwood, I would speak only with my 
mirror.

>
>I do believe (and have expressed it here before), that all of us have some 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>example).

And yet... most readers here, I'd imagine, can give examples of rudeness, 
bullying, improper use of power or inappropriate behavior they've seen.  
Since such behavior appears to be so common it might be wise to prepare 
for it, just as one prepares for careless drivers by looking both ways 
before crossing the street.

In my case it is rather simple... what I do, what I know how to do can 
make money for someone else.  I give something of value, they get 
something of value... and as a result they give something of value (money) 
and I get that.  If they want more of value from me then it is easy to 
get... by giving more of value to me, the equations must remain balanced.

If a company wants someone to bleed for them then either they'll have to 
pay me my bleeding-rate or they'll have to hire a haemophiliac.

>
>But stupidity is something else. Management don't have a monopoly on it.

Some say it is the secondmost common element, after hydrogen, and against 
it the very gods contend in vain.  I do not contest that... what I guard 
against is the contradictory attitude of 'An organisation that tries to 
get the most of a commodity for the least amount of expense is doing Good 
Business, an individual who tries to get the most amount of reimbursement 
for the least amount of effort expended is showing a Bad Attitude.'

It works both ways... and if I don't see it working both ways then I 
consult.  I've been consulting for a few decades, now.

[snip]

>I've decided not to argue this any more (you may consider the motion that 
>'Management sucks...' carried :-)) because it has to be subjective, and it 
>has to depend on variables like corporate culture and the location where you 
>are working.

I do not say that 'management sucks', Mr Dashwood... I have said that 
based on my experiences, admittedly in a series of what are most likely 
'sick shops', I have made observations and adopted behaviors to maximise 
benefits to my clients and myself based thereon.

>
>I simply hope you find better managers. :-)

Were I to find a group whose primary motive seemed to be 'how do we make 
things better for everyone?' I might even consider joining them... if 
they'd have the likes of me, of course, knowing that I am ready, at any 
sign of maltreatement, to barge into a Corner Office and bellow 'What the 
*hell* are you doing to My People?  You don't treat My People that way!'

DD

[unchanged remainder snipped]
```

```
Mr Maclean, all the rest of me is slipping into its dotage... perhaps

of 'post hoc, ergo propter hoc' at such remove may, perhaps, be a 
difficult thing best accomplished by those more energetic than I.
```

```
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Eh?  A clear and unambiguous use of the subjunctive is 'too easy a let 
out'?  This seems to make as much sense as 'Yes, this point was addressed 
specifically... but for the nonce I'll ignore that, just for... some

Some time after that, perhaps, the schools took up teaching something
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2006-03-07T03:55:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1141732559.882829.262780@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<dueqlr$ous$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com> <ducals$ohs$1@reader2.panix.com> <1141508228.173271.26940@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <dueqlr$ous$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <1141508228.173271.26940@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com>,
> Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> reason or another.'

Two points:

1. The word 'nonce' has a meaning in the UK which one might take as
offensive (certainly permitting of litigation).

2. In the UK, comedy show participants lambasting public individuals
often prefix their allegations with the word 'allegedly'. In law the
use of allegedly to distance the allegation from the speaker would not
be sufficient defence to a lawsuit. Similarly, 'might' is insufficient
to get you off of the hook.

>
> >As for the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

I thought the web was used to access porn?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-03-07T12:38:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dujus8$7me$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com> <1141508228.173271.26940@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <dueqlr$ous$1@reader2.panix.com> <1141732559.882829.262780@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1141732559.882829.262780@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,
Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>offensive (certainly permitting of litigation).

Meaning, as Wittgenstein tells it, is the result of interpretation, Mr 
Maclean; that being the case it might be wiser to request the author's 
intent before one gets pissed over it.

(note to speakers of other dialects: 'piss' is a verb in English, derived 
from the Old French 'pissier', 'to urinate'; as a colloquialism the 
Britspeakers tend to use it to indicate intoxication or dismissal while 
the USians tend to use it to indicate anger... my use above was intended 
to be ambiguous.)

>
>2. In the UK, comedy show participants lambasting public individuals
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>to get you off of the hook.

I'll keep that in mind when I participate in a UK comedy show or in legal 
proceedings; rest assured that it was my intent in using 'might' above as 
a clear and unambiguous indicator that I was employing the subjunctive 
mood.  My apologies for having been so obscure that such an indication 
required further explication, of course.

>
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>I thought the web was used to access porn?

I barely know what *I* think, Mr Maclean, let alone anyone else... but 
that you might see something as a tool towards a particular end does not, 
I believe, limit the actual or possible use of said tool towards said 
end... a wide, wonderful world out there, full of things to surprise and 
teach, should one be sufficiently energetic to open one's eyes and mind.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

```
On 7 Mar 2006 03:55:59 -0800, "Alistair"
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>
>1. The word 'nonce' has a meaning in the UK which one might take as
>offensive (certainly permitting of litigation).

Interesting, The Oxford English Dictionary lists this secondary
meaning.   It also lists as primary the meaning that we are familiar
with on this side of the pond.

Unless we are wanting to avoid Beavis & Butthead type chuckles, we
should be able to use the word in its primary meaning.   (Don't talk
about putting a fag to your lips in a book if you want Americans to
get the same meaning as Brits get).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: As Was Done With Training, Perhaps

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2006-03-07T22:37:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lWnPf.119467$H%4.98238@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9p5r025oqforoeq0esalejo6ic5mlprn16@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<du739j$qek$1@reader2.panix.com> <ducals$ohs$1@reader2.panix.com> <1141508228.173271.26940@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <dueqlr$ous$1@reader2.panix.com> <1141732559.882829.262780@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <9p5r025oqforoeq0esalejo6ic5mlprn16@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 7 Mar 2006 03:55:59 -0800, "Alistair"
> <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> get the same meaning as Brits get).

You picked up on an interesting word - so I did go to my Concise OED 
('64) for confirmation.

Fag -

(a) toil painfully, tire, make weary. A definition I had never heard of. 
  As a result of feeling weary after an arduous task I suppose you could 
say "I feel fagged" - but more colloquially - "I feel shagged !", or "I 
feel f....d !".

(b) a school fag - 'Tom Brown's School Days' - the senior boys had 
juniors nominated as their 'fags', clean their rooms, polish their shoes 
etc. The young kids had to run pronto when the word "Fag !" was bellowed 
out.

(I think the first version filmed was 1940 starring Cedric Hardwicke as 
the 'saintly' Headmaster Dr. Arnold and Freddie Bartholomew. Arnold 
wanted to break things like the fagging system - akin to hazing. 
Subsequent filming plus I believe a couple of BBC TV mini-series. One 
English version had Robert Newton as the headmaster. Remember him from 
'Treasure Island'- as Long John Silver, "Arrh Jim lad. Look 'e 
here....". Had an uncle who worked for a contractor to the studios 
providing lighting. Newton had a reputation for being pissed out of his 
mind. When filming 'Tom Brown....", his most telling performances were 
when he was as drunk as a skunk !).

There's even an on-line version of the book you can read :-

http://hughes.thefreelibrary.com/Tom-Browns-Schooldays

(c) Reference to cigarette - and don't know how that came about. Not 
something you would be likely to see in a novel unless the character was 
a Cockney : "You know the Cigarette Man in the 'X-Files'". "Who ?". "The 
geezer wots always got a fag 'anging out of his mowth".

Faggot or Fagot -

Bundles of sticks or wood bound together as fuel. As used to send Joan 
of Arc on her way to Heaven.

As a pair, unless the idiom has changed considerably since I left, the 
words are sort of inter-changeable. Fag generally means slang for 
cigarette; on the other hand Fag and Faggot can mean 'Boys will be boys'.

Jimmy
```

```
Huh!!!!!!!!!! I don't think midnight and noon are the same time.

But why is this so difficult? If 12:00:00.000000 AM has to be assigned to a 
day, wouldn't it be reasonable that it would be the same day as 
12:00:00.000001 AM? And that 11:59:59.999999 PM would be the previous day? 
How could anyone suggest that midnight would be 12:00:00 PM, but one second 
later would be 12:00:01 AM?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ambiguity

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g1kg029q9dqu3tgj5g7sjm4q3qadjo21jd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1140599370.807157.180590@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <m6qrv19ukijdcmdh6l805k0qm8er5tt9o9@4ax.com> <KnpLf.65759$B94.16796@pd7tw3no> <o27uv11n5ass749l8k7re234bma53r0cnu@4ax.com> <dtn7vk$e5n$1@reader2.panix.com> <lb9uv19l9ijm6jkn829m46o2icjtet4chh@4ax.com> <b419d$43ffae5c$45491d7a$6562@KNOLOGY.NET> <yj2Nf.7641$Cp4.3893@edtnps90> <120a9slhbl7j291@news.supernews.com> <UtPNf.1058$jf2.81@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
On Fri, 03 Mar 2006 04:20:04 GMT, "William Bub"
<fathafluff@hotmail.com> wrote:

>But why is this so difficult? If 12:00:00.000000 AM has to be assigned to a 
>day, wouldn't it be reasonable that it would be the same day as 
>12:00:00.000001 AM? And that 11:59:59.999999 PM would be the previous day? 

It's easier to program a clock to say 12:xx is all the same.   But
that doesn't make it right.    

>How could anyone suggest that midnight would be 12:00:00 PM, but one second 
>later would be 12:00:01 AM?

Does it matter how it happens?   It happens.   Even computer languages
don't all agree.   

Either do it right - no such thing as noon in the afternoon - or
program for the real world - assume that 12:00 pm means different
things to different people and interfaces.

But don't assume that your particular incorrect interpretation is
universally accepted, despite evidence to the contrary.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ambiguity

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-03T16:23:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<H3_Nf.8670$Ui.4349@edtnps84>`
- **References:** `<1140599370.807157.180590@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <m6qrv19ukijdcmdh6l805k0qm8er5tt9o9@4ax.com> <KnpLf.65759$B94.16796@pd7tw3no> <o27uv11n5ass749l8k7re234bma53r0cnu@4ax.com> <dtn7vk$e5n$1@reader2.panix.com> <lb9uv19l9ijm6jkn829m46o2icjtet4chh@4ax.com> <b419d$43ffae5c$45491d7a$6562@KNOLOGY.NET> <yj2Nf.7641$Cp4.3893@edtnps90> <120a9slhbl7j291@news.supernews.com> <UtPNf.1058$jf2.81@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
[Post re-ordered]
"William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:UtPNf.1058$jf2.81@twister.nyroc.rr.com...
>
> "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com> wrote in message 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Huh!!!!!!!!!! I don't think midnight and noon are the same time.

    That's not what was being suggested.

>
> But why is this so difficult? If 12:00:00.000000 AM has to be assigned to 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> second later would be 12:00:01 AM?

    The 'M' in AM and PM means noon (stands for Meridian). AM means before 
noon, and PM means after noon. So 12:00AM of January 15th means the "12:00" 
that occurs before the noon of January 15th. Well, the "12" that occurs 
before noon of January 15th is the midnight that is between January 15th and 
January 14th.

    12:00PM of January 14th means the "12:00" that occurs after noon of 
January 14th. Well, the "12" that occors after noon of January 14th is the 
midnight that is between January 14th and January 15th.

    So "Jan 15, 12:00AM" and "Jan 14, 12:00PM" refer to the same point in 
time.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ambiguity

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Robert Jones" <rjones0@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-03T14:19:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1141424387.027187.299590@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<H3_Nf.8670$Ui.4349@edtnps84>`
- **References:** `<1140599370.807157.180590@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <m6qrv19ukijdcmdh6l805k0qm8er5tt9o9@4ax.com> <KnpLf.65759$B94.16796@pd7tw3no> <o27uv11n5ass749l8k7re234bma53r0cnu@4ax.com> <dtn7vk$e5n$1@reader2.panix.com> <lb9uv19l9ijm6jkn829m46o2icjtet4chh@4ax.com> <b419d$43ffae5c$45491d7a$6562@KNOLOGY.NET> <yj2Nf.7641$Cp4.3893@edtnps90> <120a9slhbl7j291@news.supernews.com> <UtPNf.1058$jf2.81@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <H3_Nf.8670$Ui.4349@edtnps84>`

```
According to NPL (National Physical Laboratory in the UK)

see http://www.npl.co.uk/time/faq_index.html#midnight

************************************************************

12: Is midnight 12 a.m. or 12 p.m.?

There is no confusion when using the words 12 noon (or mid-day) and 12
midnight, although the use of 12 midnight can raise the question of
'which day?'. To avoid confusion in, for example, an insurance
certificate, it is always better to use the 24-hour clock, when 12:00
is 12 noon and, for example, 24:00 Sunday or 00:00 Monday both mean 12
midnight Sunday/Monday. It is common in transport timetables to use
23:59 Sunday or 00:01 Monday (in this example), or 11:59 p.m. or 12:01
a.m., to further reduce confusion.

There are no standards established for the meaning of 12 a.m. and 12
p.m. It is often said that 12 a.m. Monday is midnight on Monday morning
and 12 p.m. is mid-day. This puts all the times beginning with 12 and
ending with a.m. in the same one-hour block, similarly with those
ending with p.m. It can also be argued that by the time you have seen a
clock showing 12:00 at mid-day it is already post meridiem, and
similarly at midnight it is already ante meridiem. Times in the first
hour of the day are sometimes given as, for example, 00:47 a.m., with
00:00 a.m. corresponding to midnight, but with a time twelve hours
later given as 12:47 p.m.

Another convention sometimes used is that, since 12 noon is by
definition neither ante meridiem (before noon) nor post meridiem (after
noon), then 12 a.m. refers to midnight at the start of the specified
day (00:00) and 12 p.m. to midnight at the end of that day (24:00).
Given this ambiguity, the terms 12 a.m. and 12 p.m. should be avoided.

*****************************

Robert
```
